title: Charles Dickens

infobox.value: Charles John Huffam Dickens  (1812-02-07)7 February 1812  [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), Hampshire, England

* *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")* * *[Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist "Oliver Twist")* * *[Nicholas Nickleby](/wiki/Nicholas_Nickleby "Nicholas Nickleby")* * *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol "A Christmas Carol")* * *[David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield "David Copperfield")* * *[Bleak House](/wiki/Bleak_House "Bleak House")* * *[Little Dorrit](/wiki/Little_Dorrit "Little Dorrit")* * *[A Tale of Two Cities](/wiki/A_Tale_of_Two_Cities "A Tale of Two Cities")* * *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations "Great Expectations")*

* [Charles Dickens Jr.](/wiki/Charles_Dickens_Jr. "Charles Dickens Jr.") * [Mary Dickens](/wiki/Mary_Dickens "Mary Dickens") * [Kate Perugini](/wiki/Kate_Perugini "Kate Perugini") * [Walter Landor Dickens](/wiki/Walter_Landor_Dickens "Walter Landor Dickens") * [Francis Dickens](/wiki/Francis_Dickens "Francis Dickens") * [Alfred D'Orsay Tennyson Dickens](/wiki/Alfred_D%27Orsay_Tennyson_Dickens "Alfred D'Orsay Tennyson Dickens") * [Sydney Smith Haldimand Dickens](/wiki/Sydney_Smith_Haldimand_Dickens "Sydney Smith Haldimand Dickens") * [Henry Fielding Dickens](/wiki/Henry_Fielding_Dickens "Henry Fielding Dickens") * [Dora Annie Dickens](/wiki/Dora_Annie_Dickens "Dora Annie Dickens") * [Edward Dickens](/wiki/Edward_Dickens "Edward Dickens")

([/ˈdɪkɪnz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Dickens.oga "File:En-us-Dickens.oga"); 7 February 1812 – 9 June 1870) was an English [writer](/wiki/Writer "Writer") and [journalist](/wiki/Journalist "Journalist"). He created some of literature's best-known fictional characters, and is regarded by many as the greatest novelist of the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era").[[1]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-1) His works enjoyed unprecedented popularity during his lifetime and, by the 20th century, critics and scholars had recognised him as a literary genius. His novels and short stories are widely read today.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Charles John Huffam Dickens** ([/ˈdɪkɪnz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Dickens.oga "File:En-us-Dickens.oga"); 7 February 1812 – 9 June 1870) was an English [writer](/wiki/Writer "Writer") and [journalist](/wiki/Journalist "Journalist"). He created some of literature's best-known fictional characters, and is regarded by many as the greatest novelist of the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era").[[1]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-1) His works enjoyed unprecedented popularity during his lifetime and, by the 20th century, critics and scholars had recognised him as a literary genius. His novels and short stories are widely read today.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Born in [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), Dickens left school at age 12 to work in a boot-blacking factory when his father [John](/wiki/John_Dickens "John Dickens") was incarcerated in a [debtors' prison](/wiki/Debtors%27_prison "Debtors' prison"). After three years, he returned to school before beginning his literary career as a journalist. Dickens edited a weekly journal for 20 years; wrote [15 novels, 5 novellas](/wiki/Charles_Dickens_bibliography "Charles Dickens bibliography"), hundreds of short stories and nonfiction articles; lectured and performed [readings](/wiki/Penny_reading "Penny reading") extensively; was a tireless letter writer; and campaigned vigorously for [children's rights](/wiki/Children%27s_rights "Children's rights"), education and other social reforms.

Dickens's literary success began with the 1836 [serial](/wiki/Serial_(literature) "Serial (literature)") publication of *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")*, a publishing phenomenon—thanks largely to the introduction of the character [Sam Weller](/wiki/Sam_Weller_(character) "Sam Weller (character)") in the fourth episode—that sparked *Pickwick* merchandise and spin-offs. Within a few years, Dickens had become an international literary celebrity, famous for his humour, satire and keen observation of character and society. His novels, most of them published in monthly or weekly instalments, pioneered the serial publication of narrative fiction, which became the dominant Victorian mode for novel publication.[[4]](#cite_note-Grossman_2012_54-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Lodge_2002_118-5) [Cliffhanger](/wiki/Cliffhanger "Cliffhanger") endings in his serial publications kept readers in suspense.[[6]](#cite_note-NewYorker-6) The installment format allowed Dickens to evaluate his audience's reaction, and he often modified his plot and character development based on such feedback.[[5]](#cite_note-Lodge_2002_118-5) For example, when his wife's [podiatrist](/wiki/Podiatrist "Podiatrist") expressed distress at the way Miss Mowcher in *[David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield "David Copperfield")* seemed to reflect her own disabilities, Dickens improved the character with positive features.[[7]](#cite_note-7) His plots were carefully constructed and he often wove elements from topical events into his narratives.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Masses of the illiterate poor would individually pay a [halfpenny](/wiki/Halfpenny_(British_pre-decimal_coin) "Halfpenny (British pre-decimal coin)") to have each new monthly episode read to them, opening up and inspiring a new class of readers.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

His 1843 novella *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol "A Christmas Carol")* remains especially popular and continues to inspire adaptations in every creative medium. *[Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist "Oliver Twist")* and *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations "Great Expectations")* are also frequently adapted and, like many of his novels, evoke images of early Victorian [London](/wiki/London "London"). His 1853 novel *[Bleak House](/wiki/Bleak_House "Bleak House")*, a satire on the judicial system, helped support a reformist movement that culminated in the [1870s legal reform](/wiki/Judicature_Acts "Judicature Acts") in England. *[A Tale of Two Cities](/wiki/A_Tale_of_Two_Cities "A Tale of Two Cities")* (1859; set in London and Paris) is regarded as his best-known work of historical fiction. The most famous celebrity of his era, he undertook, in response to public demand, a series of public reading tours in the later part of his career.[[10]](#cite_note-Garratt-10) The term *Dickensian* is used to describe something that is reminiscent of Dickens and his writings, such as poor social or working conditions, or comically repulsive characters.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Early life

Main article: [Dickens family](/wiki/Dickens_family "Dickens family")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/CharlesDickens_house_Portsmouth.JPG/250px-CharlesDickens_house_Portsmouth.JPG)](/wiki/File:CharlesDickens_house_Portsmouth.JPG)

Charles Dickens's birthplace, 393 Commercial Road, [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), Hampshire

[![photograph](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/ChathamOrdnanceTerrCrop.jpg/250px-ChathamOrdnanceTerrCrop.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChathamOrdnanceTerrCrop.jpg)

2 Ordnance Terrace (house on the left), [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Medway "Chatham, Medway"), Kent, Dickens's home from 1817 to 1821

Charles John Huffam Dickens was born on 7 February 1812 at 1 Mile End Terrace (now 393 Commercial Road), [Landport](/wiki/Landport "Landport") in [Portsea Island](/wiki/Portsea_Island "Portsea Island") ([Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth")), [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"), the second of eight children of [Elizabeth Dickens](/wiki/Elizabeth_Dickens "Elizabeth Dickens") (née Barrow; 1789–1863) and [John Dickens](/wiki/John_Dickens "John Dickens") (1785–1851). His father was a clerk in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") Pay Office and was temporarily stationed in the district. He asked Christopher Huffam, rigger in the Royal Navy and head of an established firm, to act as godfather to Charles.[[13]](#cite_note-West1999-13) Huffam is thought to be the inspiration for Paul Dombey, the owner of a shipping company in Dickens's novel *[Dombey and Son](/wiki/Dombey_and_Son "Dombey and Son")* (1848).[[13]](#cite_note-West1999-13)

In January 1815 John Dickens was called back to London, and the family moved to Norfolk Street, [Fitzrovia](/wiki/Fitzrovia "Fitzrovia").[[14]](#cite_note-Callow2012p5-14) When Charles was four they relocated to [Sheerness](/wiki/Sheerness "Sheerness") and thence to [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Kent "Chatham, Kent"), Kent, where he spent his formative years until the age of 11.[[15]](#cite_note-Callow2012p9-15) His early life seems to have been idyllic, though he thought himself a "very small and not-over-particularly-taken-care-of boy".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Charles spent time outdoors, but also read voraciously, including the [picaresque novels](/wiki/Picaresque_novel "Picaresque novel") of [Tobias Smollett](/wiki/Tobias_Smollett "Tobias Smollett") and [Henry Fielding](/wiki/Henry_Fielding "Henry Fielding"), as well as *[Robinson Crusoe](/wiki/Robinson_Crusoe "Robinson Crusoe")* and *[Gil Blas](/wiki/Gil_Blas "Gil Blas")*. He read and re-read *[The Arabian Nights](/wiki/The_Arabian_Nights "The Arabian Nights")* and the Collected Farces of [Elizabeth Inchbald](/wiki/Elizabeth_Inchbald "Elizabeth Inchbald").[[17]](#cite_note-Callow2012p7-17) Aged seven, he first saw [Joseph Grimaldi](/wiki/Joseph_Grimaldi "Joseph Grimaldi")—the father of modern [clowning](/wiki/Clown "Clown")—perform at the Star Theatre in [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent"), Kent.[[18]](#cite_note-18) He later imitated Grimaldi's clowning on several occasions, and would also edit the *[Memoirs of Joseph Grimaldi](/wiki/Memoirs_of_Joseph_Grimaldi "Memoirs of Joseph Grimaldi")*.[[19]](#cite_note-Forster65-19)[[nb 1]](#cite_note-22) He retained poignant memories of childhood, helped by an excellent memory of people and events, which he used in his writing.[[22]](#cite_note-23) His father's brief work as a clerk in the Navy Pay Office afforded him a few years of private education, first at a [dame school](/wiki/Dame_school "Dame school") and then at a school run by William Giles, a [dissenter](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters"), in Chatham.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

[![drawing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Dickens-at-the-Blacking-Warehouse.jpg/250px-Dickens-at-the-Blacking-Warehouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dickens-at-the-Blacking-Warehouse.jpg)

An illustration by Fred Bernard of Dickens at work in a shoe-blacking factory after his father had been sent to the [Marshalsea](/wiki/Marshalsea "Marshalsea"), published in the 1892 edition of Forster's *Life of Charles Dickens*[[24]](#cite_note-25)

This period came to an end in June 1822, when John Dickens was recalled to Navy Pay Office headquarters at [Somerset House](/wiki/Somerset_House "Somerset House") and the family (except for Charles, who stayed behind to finish his final term at school) moved to [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town") in London.[[25]](#cite_note-Callow2009p13-26) The family had left Kent amidst rapidly mounting debts and, living beyond his means,[[26]](#cite_note-27) John Dickens was forced by his creditors into the [Marshalsea](/wiki/Marshalsea "Marshalsea") [debtors' prison](/wiki/Debtors%27_prison "Debtors' prison") in [Southwark](/wiki/Southwark "Southwark"), London in 1824. His wife and youngest children joined him there, as was the practice at the time. Charles, then 12 years old, boarded with Elizabeth Roylance, a family friend, at 112 College Place, Camden Town.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Mrs Roylance was "a reduced impoverished old lady, long known to our family", whom Dickens later immortalised, "with a few alterations and embellishments", as "Mrs Pipchin" in *Dombey and Son*. Later, he lived in a back-attic in the house of an agent for the [Insolvent Court](/wiki/Insolvency "Insolvency"), Archibald Russell, "a fat, good-natured, kind old gentleman ... with a quiet old wife" and lame son, in [Lant Street](/wiki/Lant_Street "Lant Street") in Southwark.[[28]](#cite_note-29) They provided the inspiration for the Garlands in *[The Old Curiosity Shop](/wiki/The_Old_Curiosity_Shop "The Old Curiosity Shop")*.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

On Sundays—with his sister [Frances](/wiki/Fanny_Dickens "Fanny Dickens"), free from her studies at the [Royal Academy of Music](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Music "Royal Academy of Music")—he spent the day at the Marshalsea.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Dickens later used the prison as a setting in *[Little Dorrit](/wiki/Little_Dorrit "Little Dorrit")*. To pay for his board and to help his family, Dickens was forced to leave school and work ten-hour days at Warren's [Blacking](/wiki/Shoe_polish "Shoe polish") Warehouse, on Hungerford Stairs, near the present [Charing Cross railway station](/wiki/Charing_Cross_railway_station "Charing Cross railway station"), where he earned six [shillings](/wiki/Shilling "Shilling") a week pasting labels on pots of boot blacking. The strenuous and often harsh working conditions made a lasting impression on Dickens and later influenced his fiction and essays, becoming the foundation of his interest in the reform of socio-economic and labour conditions, the rigours of which he believed were unfairly borne by the poor. He later wrote that he wondered "how I could have been so easily cast away at such an age".[[31]](#cite_note-Foster23-32) As he recalled to [John Forster](/wiki/John_Forster_(biographer) "John Forster (biographer)") (from *Life of Charles Dickens*):

> The blacking-warehouse was the last house on the left-hand side of the way, at old Hungerford Stairs. It was a crazy, tumble-down old house, abutting of course on the river, and literally overrun with rats. Its wainscoted rooms, and its rotten floors and staircase, and the old grey rats swarming down in the cellars, and the sound of their squeaking and scuffling coming up the stairs at all times, and the dirt and decay of the place, rise up visibly before me, as if I were there again. The counting-house was on the first floor, looking over the coal-barges and the river. There was a recess in it, in which I was to sit and work. My work was to cover the pots of paste-blacking; first with a piece of oil-paper, and then with a piece of blue paper; to tie them round with a string; and then to clip the paper close and neat, all round, until it looked as smart as a pot of ointment from an apothecary's shop. When a certain number of grosses of pots had attained this pitch of perfection, I was to paste on each a printed label, and then go on again with more pots. Two or three other boys were kept at similar duty down-stairs on similar wages. One of them came up, in a ragged apron and a paper cap, on the first Monday morning, to show me the trick of using the string and tying the knot. His name was Bob Fagin; and I took the liberty of using his name, long afterwards, in Oliver Twist.[[31]](#cite_note-Foster23-32)

When the warehouse was moved to Chandos Street in the smart, busy district of [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden"), the boys worked in a room in which the window gave onto the street. Small audiences gathered and watched them at work—in Dickens's biographer [Simon Callow](/wiki/Simon_Callow "Simon Callow")'s estimation, the public display was "a new refinement added to his misery".[[32]](#cite_note-Callow2009p25-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Courtyard_of_the_former_Marshalsea_prison%2C_1897_%282%29.png/250px-Courtyard_of_the_former_Marshalsea_prison%2C_1897_%282%29.png)](/wiki/File:Courtyard_of_the_former_Marshalsea_prison,_1897_(2).png)

The [Marshalsea](/wiki/Marshalsea "Marshalsea") around 1897, after it had closed. Dickens based several of his characters on the experience of seeing his father in the debtors' prison, most notably [Amy Dorrit](/wiki/Little_Dorrit "Little Dorrit") from *Little Dorrit*.

A few months after his imprisonment, John Dickens's mother, Elizabeth Dickens, died and bequeathed him £450. On the expectation of this legacy, Dickens was released from prison. Under the [Insolvent Debtors Act](/wiki/Insolvent_Debtors_(England)_Act_1813 "Insolvent Debtors (England) Act 1813"), Dickens arranged for payment of his creditors, and he and his family left the Marshalsea,[[33]](#cite_note-34) for the home of Mrs Roylance.

Charles's mother, Elizabeth Dickens, did not immediately support his removal from the boot-blacking warehouse. This influenced Dickens's view that a father should rule the family and a mother find her proper sphere inside the home: "I never afterwards forgot, I never shall forget, I never can forget, that my mother was warm for my being sent back." His mother's failure to request his return was a factor in his dissatisfied attitude towards women.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Righteous indignation stemming from his own situation and the conditions under which [working-class](/wiki/Working-class "Working-class") people lived became major themes of his works, and it was this unhappy period in his youth to which he alluded in his favourite and most [autobiographical novel](/wiki/Autobiographical_novel "Autobiographical novel"), *[David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield "David Copperfield")*:[[35]](#cite_note-36) "I had no advice, no counsel, no encouragement, no consolation, no assistance, no support, of any kind, from anyone, that I can call to mind, as I hope to go to heaven!"[[36]](#cite_note-Wilson61-37)

Dickens was eventually sent to the Wellington House Academy in [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town"), where he remained until March 1827, having spent about two years there. He did not consider it to be a good school: "Much of the haphazard, desultory teaching, poor discipline punctuated by the headmaster's sadistic brutality, the seedy ushers and general run-down atmosphere, are embodied in Mr Creakle's Establishment in *David Copperfield*."[[36]](#cite_note-Wilson61-37)

Dickens worked at the law office of Ellis and Blackmore, attorneys, of Holborn Court, [Gray's Inn](/wiki/Gray%27s_Inn "Gray's Inn"), as a junior [clerk](/wiki/Law_clerk "Law clerk") from May 1827 to November 1828. He was a gifted mimic and impersonated those around him: clients, lawyers and clerks. Captivated with London's theatre scene, he went to theatres obsessively: he claimed that for at least three years he went to the theatre every day.[[37]](#cite_note-38) His favourite actor was [Charles Mathews](/wiki/Charles_Mathews "Charles Mathews") and Dickens learnt his "[monopolylogues](/wiki/Monopolylogue "Monopolylogue")" (farces in which Mathews played every character) by heart.[[38]](#cite_note-Callow2009p34-39) Then, having learned [Thomas Gurney](/wiki/Thomas_Gurney_(shorthand_writer) "Thomas Gurney (shorthand writer)")'s system of shorthand in his spare time, he left to become a freelance reporter. A distant relative, Thomas Charlton, was a freelance reporter at [Doctors' Commons](/wiki/Doctors%27_Commons "Doctors' Commons") and Dickens was able to share his box there to report the legal proceedings for nearly four years.[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

In 1830 Dickens met his first love, Maria Beadnell, thought to have been the model for the character Dora in *David Copperfield*. Maria's parents disapproved of the courtship and ended the relationship by sending her to school in Paris.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

## Career

### Journalism and writing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Catherine_Dickens.jpg/250px-Catherine_Dickens.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catherine_Dickens.jpg)

Catherine Hogarth Dickens by [Samuel Laurence](/wiki/Samuel_Laurence "Samuel Laurence") (1838). She met the author in 1834, and they became engaged the following year before marrying in April 1836.

In 1832, at the age of 20, Dickens was energetic and increasingly self-confident.[[42]](#cite_note-Callow2009p48-43) He enjoyed mimicry and popular entertainment, lacked a sense of what he wanted to become, and yet knew he wanted fame. Drawn to the theatre—he became an early member of the [Garrick Club](/wiki/Garrick_Club "Garrick Club")—he landed an acting audition at Covent Garden, where the manager [George Bartley](/wiki/George_Bartley_(comedian) "George Bartley (comedian)") and the actor [Charles Kemble](/wiki/Charles_Kemble "Charles Kemble") were to see him.[[43]](#cite_note-Tomalin1992p7-44) Dickens prepared meticulously and decided to imitate the comedian Charles Mathews, but ultimately he missed the audition because of a cold. Before another opportunity arose, he had set out on his career as a writer.[[44]](#cite_note-Tomalin1992p76-45)

In 1833 Dickens submitted his first story, "A Dinner at Poplar Walk", to the London periodical *[Monthly Magazine](/wiki/Monthly_Magazine "Monthly Magazine")*.[[45]](#cite_note-Patten(2001)16ff-46) His uncle William Barrow offered him a job on *The Mirror of Parliament* and he worked in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") for the first time early in 1832. He rented rooms at [15 Buckingham Street](/wiki/Buckingham_Street,_London "Buckingham Street, London")[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48) and later at [Furnival's Inn](/wiki/Furnival%27s_Inn "Furnival's Inn") and worked as a political journalist, reporting on [Parliamentary](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") debates, and he travelled across Britain to cover election campaigns for the *[Morning Chronicle](/wiki/Morning_Chronicle "Morning Chronicle")*.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Sketches_by_Boz_illustrated_by_George_Cruikshank_1837.jpg/250px-Sketches_by_Boz_illustrated_by_George_Cruikshank_1837.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sketches_by_Boz_illustrated_by_George_Cruikshank_1837.jpg)

[Frontispiece](/wiki/Book_frontispiece "Book frontispiece"), *Sketches by Boz*—Boz being a family nickname—written by Dickens with illustrations by [George Cruikshank](/wiki/George_Cruikshank "George Cruikshank"), 1837

His journalism, in the form of sketches in periodicals, formed his first collection of pieces, published in 1836: *[Sketches by Boz](/wiki/Sketches_by_Boz "Sketches by Boz")*—Boz being a family nickname he employed as a pseudonym for some years.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-Glancy_1999_6-51) Dickens apparently adopted it from the nickname 'Moses', which he had given to his youngest brother [Augustus Dickens](/wiki/Augustus_Dickens "Augustus Dickens"), after a character in Oliver Goldsmith's *[The Vicar of Wakefield](/wiki/The_Vicar_of_Wakefield "The Vicar of Wakefield")*. When pronounced by anyone with a head cold, "Moses" became "Boses"—later shortened to *Boz*.[[50]](#cite_note-Glancy_1999_6-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Dickens's name was considered "queer" by a contemporary critic, who wrote in 1849: "Mr Dickens, as if in revenge for his own queer name, does bestow still queerer ones upon his fictitious creations". Dickens contributed to and edited journals throughout his literary career.[[45]](#cite_note-Patten(2001)16ff-46) In January 1835, the *Morning Chronicle* launched an evening edition, under the editorship of the *Chronicle*'s music critic, [George Hogarth](/wiki/George_Hogarth "George Hogarth"). Hogarth invited him to contribute *Street Sketches* and Dickens became a regular visitor to his Fulham house—excited by Hogarth's friendship with [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott") (whom Dickens greatly admired) and enjoying the company of Hogarth's three daughters, Georgina, Mary and 19-year-old Catherine.[[52]](#cite_note-Callow2009p54-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Sam-weller-kyd.jpeg/250px-Sam-weller-kyd.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Sam-weller-kyd.jpeg)

The humorous, warm-hearted servant [Sam Weller](/wiki/Sam_Weller_(character) "Sam Weller (character)") from *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")*—a publishing phenomenon that sparked numerous spin-offs and *Pickwick* merchandise—made the 24-year-old Dickens famous.[[53]](#cite_note-Paris_Review-54)

Dickens made rapid progress professionally and socially. He began a friendship with [William Harrison Ainsworth](/wiki/William_Harrison_Ainsworth "William Harrison Ainsworth"), the author of the highwayman novel *[Rookwood](/wiki/Rookwood_(novel) "Rookwood (novel)")* (1834), whose bachelor salon in [Harrow Road](/wiki/Harrow_Road "Harrow Road") had become the meeting place for a set that included [Daniel Maclise](/wiki/Daniel_Maclise "Daniel Maclise"), [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli"), [Edward Bulwer-Lytton](/wiki/Edward_Bulwer-Lytton "Edward Bulwer-Lytton") and [George Cruikshank](/wiki/George_Cruikshank "George Cruikshank"). All these became his friends and collaborators, with the exception of Disraeli, and he met his first publisher, John Macrone, at the house.[[54]](#cite_note-Callow2012p56-55)

The success of *Sketches by Boz* led to a proposal from publishers [Chapman and Hall](/wiki/Chapman_and_Hall "Chapman and Hall") for Dickens to supply text to match [Robert Seymour](/wiki/Robert_Seymour_(illustrator) "Robert Seymour (illustrator)")'s engraved illustrations in a monthly [letterpress](/wiki/Letterpress_printing "Letterpress printing"). Seymour committed suicide after the second instalment and Dickens, who wanted to write a connected series of sketches, hired "[Phiz](/wiki/Hablot_Knight_Browne "Hablot Knight Browne")" to provide the engravings (which were reduced from four to two per instalment) for the story. The resulting story became *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")* and, although the first few episodes were not successful, the introduction of the Cockney character [Sam Weller](/wiki/Sam_Weller_(character) "Sam Weller (character)") in the fourth episode (the first to be illustrated by Phiz) marked a sharp climb in its popularity.[[55]](#cite_note-Callow2012p60-56) The final instalment sold 40,000 copies.[[45]](#cite_note-Patten(2001)16ff-46) On the impact of the character, *[The Paris Review](/wiki/The_Paris_Review "The Paris Review")* stated in 2015, "arguably the most historic bump in English publishing is the Sam Weller Bump."[[53]](#cite_note-Paris_Review-54) A publishing phenomenon, [John Sutherland](/wiki/John_Sutherland_(author) "John Sutherland (author)"), Professor of Modern English Literature at University College London, called *The Pickwick Papers* "[t]he most important single novel of the Victorian era".[[56]](#cite_note-57) The unprecedented success led to numerous spin-offs and merchandise including *Pickwick* cigars, playing cards, china figurines, Sam Weller puzzles, Weller boot polish and joke books.[[53]](#cite_note-Paris_Review-54)

> The Sam Weller Bump testifies not merely to Dickens's comic genius but to his acumen as an "authorpreneur", a portmanteau he inhabited long before *The Economist* took it up. For a writer who made his reputation crusading against the squalor of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), Dickens was a creature of capitalism; he used everything from the powerful new printing presses to the enhanced advertising revenues to the expansion of railroads to sell more books. Dickens ensured that his books were available in cheap bindings for the lower orders as well as in morocco-and-gilt for people of quality; his ideal readership included everyone from the pickpockets who read *Oliver Twist* to Queen Victoria, who found it "exceedingly interesting".

— Nina Martyris, How *The Pickwick Papers* Launched Charles Dickens's Career, *The Paris Review*, April 2015[[53]](#cite_note-Paris_Review-54)

On its impact on mass culture, Nicholas Dames in *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* writes, "'Literature' is not a big enough category for *Pickwick*. It defined its own, a new one that we have learned to call 'entertainment'."[[57]](#cite_note-58) In November 1836 Dickens accepted the position of editor of *[Bentley's Miscellany](/wiki/Bentley%27s_Miscellany "Bentley's Miscellany")*, a position he held for three years, until he fell out with the owner.[[58]](#cite_note-59) In 1836, as he finished the last instalments of *The Pickwick Papers*, he began writing the beginning instalments of *[Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist "Oliver Twist")*—writing as many as 90 pages a month—while continuing work on *Bentley's* and also writing four plays, the production of which he oversaw. *Oliver Twist*, published in 1838, became one of Dickens's better known stories and was the first Victorian novel with a child [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist").[[59]](#cite_note-Smiley12ff-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Charles_Dickens_by_Daniel_Maclise.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens_by_Daniel_Maclise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens_by_Daniel_Maclise.jpg)

*[Portrait of Charles Dickens](/wiki/Portrait_of_Charles_Dickens "Portrait of Charles Dickens")* by [Daniel Maclise](/wiki/Daniel_Maclise "Daniel Maclise"), 1839

On 2 April 1836, after a one-year engagement, and between episodes two and three of *The Pickwick Papers*, Dickens married [Catherine Thomson Hogarth](/wiki/Catherine_Dickens "Catherine Dickens") (1815–1879), the daughter of George Hogarth, editor of the *[Evening Chronicle](/wiki/Evening_Chronicle "Evening Chronicle")*.[[60]](#cite_note-Schlicke1999-61) They were married in [St Luke's Church](/wiki/St_Luke%27s_Church,_Chelsea "St Luke's Church, Chelsea"), [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea,_London "Chelsea, London"), London.[[61]](#cite_note-62) After a brief honeymoon in [Chalk](/wiki/Chalk,_Kent "Chalk, Kent") in Kent, the couple returned to lodgings at [Furnival's Inn](/wiki/Furnival%27s_Inn "Furnival's Inn").[[62]](#cite_note-63) The first of [their ten children](/wiki/Dickens_family "Dickens family"), Charles, was born in January 1837 and a few months later the family set up [home in Bloomsbury](/wiki/Charles_Dickens_Museum "Charles Dickens Museum") at 48 Doughty Street, London (on which Charles had a three-year lease at £80 a year) from 25 March 1837 until December 1839.[[60]](#cite_note-Schlicke1999-61)[[63]](#cite_note-64) Dickens's younger brother [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_Dickens "Frederick Dickens") and Catherine's 17-year-old sister [Mary Hogarth](/wiki/Mary_Hogarth "Mary Hogarth") moved in with them. Dickens became very attached to Mary, and she died in his arms after a brief illness in 1837. Unusually for Dickens, as a consequence of his shock, he stopped working, and he and Catherine stayed at a little farm on [Hampstead Heath](/wiki/Hampstead_Heath "Hampstead Heath") for a fortnight. Dickens idealised Mary; the character he fashioned after her, [Rose Maylie](/wiki/Rose_Maylie "Rose Maylie"), he found he could not now kill, as he had planned, in his fiction,[[64]](#cite_note-Callow2012p74-65) and, according to Ackroyd, he drew on memories of her for his later descriptions of [Little Nell](/wiki/Little_Nell_(Dickens) "Little Nell (Dickens)") and Florence Dombey.[[65]](#cite_note-66) His grief was so great that he was unable to meet the deadline for the June instalment of *The Pickwick Papers* and had to cancel the *Oliver Twist* instalment that month as well.[[59]](#cite_note-Smiley12ff-60) The time in Hampstead was the occasion for a growing bond between Dickens and John Forster to develop; Forster soon became his unofficial business manager and the first to read his work.[[66]](#cite_note-Callow2012p77-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Dolly_Varden_by_William_Powell_Frith.jpg/250px-Dolly_Varden_by_William_Powell_Frith.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dolly_Varden_by_William_Powell_Frith.jpg)

*Barnaby Rudge* was Dickens's first popular failure but the character of Dolly Varden—pictured in an [1842 oil painting](/wiki/Dolly_Varden_(painting) "Dolly Varden (painting)") by [William Powell Frith](/wiki/William_Powell_Frith "William Powell Frith")—"pretty, witty, sexy, became central to numerous theatrical adaptations".[[67]](#cite_note-Callow2012p97-68)

His success as a novelist continued. The young [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") read both *Oliver Twist* and *The Pickwick Papers*, staying up until midnight to discuss them.[[68]](#cite_note-69) *[Nicholas Nickleby](/wiki/Nicholas_Nickleby "Nicholas Nickleby")* (1838–39), *[The Old Curiosity Shop](/wiki/The_Old_Curiosity_Shop "The Old Curiosity Shop")* (1840–41) and, finally, his first historical novel, *[Barnaby Rudge: A Tale of the Riots of 'Eighty](/wiki/Barnaby_Rudge "Barnaby Rudge")*, as part of the *[Master Humphrey's Clock](/wiki/Master_Humphrey%27s_Clock "Master Humphrey's Clock")* series (1840–41), were all published in monthly instalments before being made into books.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Dickens's biographer [Peter Ackroyd](/wiki/Peter_Ackroyd "Peter Ackroyd") has called *Barnaby Rudge* "one of Dickens's most neglected, but most rewarding, novels".[[70]](#cite_note-71) The poet [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") read *Barnaby Rudge*, and the novel's talking raven, Grip, who Dickens named after his own talking pet raven [Grip](/wiki/Grip_(raven) "Grip (raven)"), inspired in part Poe's 1845 poem "[The Raven](/wiki/The_Raven "The Raven")".[[71]](#cite_note-72) Three [ravens of the Tower of London](/wiki/Ravens_of_the_Tower_of_London "Ravens of the Tower of London") have been named after Grip, the latest in 2012 to mark the bicentenary of Dickens's birth.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

In the midst of all his activity during this period, there was discontent with his publishers and John Macrone was bought off, while [Richard Bentley](/wiki/Richard_Bentley_(publisher) "Richard Bentley (publisher)") signed over all his rights in *Oliver Twist*. Other signs of a certain restlessness and discontent emerged; in [Broadstairs](/wiki/Broadstairs "Broadstairs") he flirted with Eleanor Picken, the young fiancée of his solicitor's best friend and one night grabbed her and ran with her down to the sea. He declared they were both to drown there in the "sad sea waves". She finally got free, and afterwards kept her distance. In June 1841, he precipitously set out on a two-month tour of Scotland and then, in September 1841, telegraphed Forster that he had decided to go to America.[[73]](#cite_note-Callow2012p98-74) His weekly periodical *Master Humphrey's Clock* ended, though Dickens was still keen on the idea of the weekly magazine, an appreciation that had begun with his childhood reading of [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson")'s *[The Idler](/wiki/The_Idler_(1758%E2%80%931760) "The Idler (1758–1760)")* and the 18th-century magazines *[Tatler](/wiki/Tatler_(1709_journal) "Tatler (1709 journal)")* and *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")*.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

Dickens was perturbed by the return to power of the Tories, whom he described as "people whom, politically, I despise and abhor."[[75]](#cite_note-Slater2009p167-76) He had been tempted to stand for the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") in Reading, but decided against it due to financial straits.[[75]](#cite_note-Slater2009p167-76) He wrote three anti-Tory verse satires ("The Fine Old English Gentleman", "The Quack Doctor's Proclamation" and "Subjects for Painters") which were published in *[The Examiner](/wiki/The_Examiner_(1808%E2%80%9386) "The Examiner (1808–86)")*.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### First visit to the United States

On 22 January 1842, Dickens and his wife arrived in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), Massachusetts, aboard the [RMS *Britannia*](/wiki/RMS_Britannia "RMS Britannia") during their first trip to the United States and Canada.[[77]](#cite_note-78) At this time [Georgina Hogarth](/wiki/Georgina_Hogarth "Georgina Hogarth"), another sister of Catherine, joined the Dickens household, now living at Devonshire Terrace, [Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone "Marylebone") to care for the young family they had left behind.[[78]](#cite_note-79) She remained with them as housekeeper, organiser, adviser and friend until Dickens's death in 1870.[[79]](#cite_note-Smith10ff-80) Dickens modelled the character of [Agnes Wickfield](/wiki/Agnes_Wickfield "Agnes Wickfield") after Georgina and Mary.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Charles_Dickens_sketch_1842.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens_sketch_1842.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens_sketch_1842.jpg)

A sketch of Dickens in 1842 during his first American tour. Sketch of Dickens's sister Fanny, bottom left

He described his impressions in a [travelogue](/wiki/Travel_literature "Travel literature"), *[American Notes for General Circulation](/wiki/American_Notes "American Notes")*. In it Dickens includes a powerful condemnation of slavery which he had attacked as early as *The Pickwick Papers*, correlating the emancipation of the poor in England with the abolition of slavery abroad[[81]](#cite_note-82) citing newspaper accounts of runaway slaves disfigured by their masters. In spite of the abolitionist sentiments gleaned from his trip to America, some modern commentators have pointed out inconsistencies in Dickens's views on racial inequality. For instance, he has been criticised for his subsequent acquiescence in Governor [Edward John Eyre](/wiki/Edward_John_Eyre "Edward John Eyre")'s harsh crackdown during the 1860s [Morant Bay rebellion](/wiki/Morant_Bay_rebellion "Morant Bay rebellion") in Jamaica and his failure to join other British progressives in condemning it.[[82]](#cite_note-83) From [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia"), Dickens returned to Washington, D.C., and started a trek westwards, with brief pauses in [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati") and [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"). While there he expressed a desire to see an American prairie before returning east. A group of 13 men then set out with Dickens to visit Looking Glass Prairie, a trip 30 miles into [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois").

During his American visit, Dickens spent a month in New York City, giving lectures, raising [the question of international copyright laws](/wiki/History_of_copyright_law "History of copyright law") and the pirating of his work in America.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85) He persuaded a group of 25 writers, headed by [Washington Irving](/wiki/Washington_Irving "Washington Irving"), to sign a petition for him to take to the [US Congress](/wiki/US_Congress "US Congress"), but the press were generally hostile to this, saying that he should be grateful for his popularity and that it was mercenary to complain about his work being pirated.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

The popularity he gained caused a shift in his self-perception according to the critic Kate Flint, who writes that he "found himself a cultural commodity, and its circulation had passed out his control", causing him to become interested in and delve into themes of public and personal personas in the next novels.[[86]](#cite_note-flint35-87) She writes that he assumed a role of "influential commentator", publicly and in his fiction, evident in his next few books.[[86]](#cite_note-flint35-87) His trip to the US ended with a trip to Canada—Niagara Falls, Toronto, [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"), and Montreal—where he appeared on stage in light comedies.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

### Return to England

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Portrait_of_Charles_John_Huffman_Dickens.png/250px-Portrait_of_Charles_John_Huffman_Dickens.png)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Charles_John_Huffman_Dickens.png)

Dickens's portrait by [Margaret Gillies](/wiki/Margaret_Gillies "Margaret Gillies"), 1843. Painted during the period when he was writing *A Christmas Carol*, it was in the [Royal Academy of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts")' 1844 summer exhibition. After viewing it there, [Elizabeth Barrett Browning](/wiki/Elizabeth_Barrett_Browning "Elizabeth Barrett Browning") said that it showed Dickens with "the dust and mud of humanity about him, notwithstanding those eagle eyes".[[88]](#cite_note-Brown-89)

Soon after his return to England, Dickens began work on the first of his Christmas stories, *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol "A Christmas Carol")*, written in 1843, which was followed by *[The Chimes](/wiki/The_Chimes "The Chimes")* in 1844 and *[The Cricket on the Hearth](/wiki/The_Cricket_on_the_Hearth "The Cricket on the Hearth")* in 1845. Of these, *A Christmas Carol* was most popular and, tapping into an old tradition, did much to promote a renewed enthusiasm for the joys of Christmas in Britain and America.[[89]](#cite_note-90) The seeds for the story became planted in Dickens's mind during a trip to Manchester to witness the conditions of the manufacturing workers there. This, along with scenes he had recently witnessed at the Field Lane [Ragged School](/wiki/Ragged_School "Ragged School"), caused Dickens to resolve to "strike a sledge hammer blow" for the poor. As the idea for the story took shape and the writing began in earnest, Dickens became engrossed in the book. He later wrote that as the tale unfolded he "wept and laughed, and wept again" as he "walked about the black streets of London fifteen or twenty miles many a night when all sober folks had gone to bed".[[90]](#cite_note-91)

Between 1843 and 1844 *[Martin Chuzzlewit](/wiki/Martin_Chuzzlewit "Martin Chuzzlewit")*, the last of his [picaresque novels](/wiki/Picaresque_novel "Picaresque novel"), was serialised. It includes the character of [Sarah Gamp](/wiki/Sarah_Gamp "Sarah Gamp"), a nurse who is dissolute, sloppy and generally drunk, and also features one of the first literary [private detective](/wiki/Private_detective "Private detective") characters, Mr Nadgett.[[91]](#cite_note-92) After living briefly in Italy (1844), Dickens travelled to Switzerland (1846), where he began work on *[Dombey and Son](/wiki/Dombey_and_Son "Dombey and Son")* (1846–48).[[92]](#cite_note-93)

At about this time, he was made aware of a large embezzlement at the firm where his brother, [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus_Dickens "Augustus Dickens"), worked (John Chapman & Co). It had been carried out by [Thomas Powell](/wiki/Thomas_Powell_(1809-1887) "Thomas Powell (1809-1887)"), a clerk, who was on friendly terms with Dickens and who had acted as mentor to Augustus when he started work. Powell was also an author and poet and knew many of the famous writers of the day. After further fraudulent activities, Powell fled to New York and published a book called *The Living Authors of England* with a chapter on Charles Dickens, who was not amused by what Powell had written. One item that seemed to have annoyed him was the assertion that he had based the character of Paul Dombey (*Dombey and Son*) on Thomas Chapman, one of the principal partners at John Chapman & Co. Dickens immediately sent a letter to [Lewis Gaylord Clark](/wiki/Lewis_Gaylord_Clark "Lewis Gaylord Clark"), editor of the New York literary magazine *[The Knickerbocker](/wiki/The_Knickerbocker "The Knickerbocker")*, saying that Powell was a forger and thief. Clark published the letter in the *[New-York Tribune](/wiki/New-York_Tribune "New-York Tribune")* and several other papers picked up on the story. Powell began proceedings to sue these publications and Clark was arrested. Dickens, realising that he had acted in haste, contacted John Chapman & Co to seek written confirmation of Powell's guilt. Dickens did receive a reply confirming Powell's embezzlement, but once the directors realised this information might have to be produced in court, they refused to make further disclosures. Owing to the difficulties of providing evidence in America to support his accusations, Dickens eventually made a private settlement with Powell out of court.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

#### Philanthropy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Dulwich_College_Charity_meeting_at_the_Adelphi_Theatre_-_ILN_1856.jpg/250px-Dulwich_College_Charity_meeting_at_the_Adelphi_Theatre_-_ILN_1856.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dulwich_College_Charity_meeting_at_the_Adelphi_Theatre_-_ILN_1856.jpg)

Dickens presiding over a charity meeting to discuss the future of the [College of God's Gift](/wiki/College_of_God%27s_Gift "College of God's Gift"); from *[The Illustrated London News](/wiki/The_Illustrated_London_News "The Illustrated London News")*, March 1856

[Angela Burdett Coutts](/wiki/Angela_Burdett-Coutts,_1st_Baroness_Burdett-Coutts "Angela Burdett-Coutts, 1st Baroness Burdett-Coutts"), heir to the Coutts banking fortune, approached Dickens in May 1846 about setting up a home for the redemption of [fallen women](/wiki/Fallen_woman "Fallen woman") of the working class. Coutts envisioned a home that would replace the punitive regimes of existing institutions with a reformative environment conducive to education and proficiency in domestic household chores. After initially resisting, Dickens eventually founded the home, named [Urania Cottage](/wiki/Urania_Cottage "Urania Cottage"), in the Lime Grove area of [Shepherd's Bush](/wiki/Shepherd%27s_Bush "Shepherd's Bush"), which he managed for ten years,[[94]](#cite_note-95) setting the house rules, reviewing the accounts and interviewing prospective residents.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Emigration and marriage were central to Dickens's agenda for the women on leaving Urania Cottage, from which it is estimated that about 100 women graduated between 1847 and 1859.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

#### Religious views

As a young man, Dickens expressed a distaste for certain aspects of organised religion. In 1836, in a pamphlet titled *Sunday Under Three Heads*, he defended the people's right to pleasure, opposing a plan to prohibit games on Sundays. "Look into your churches—diminished congregations and scanty attendance. People have grown sullen and obstinate, and are becoming disgusted with the faith which condemns them to such a day as this, once in every seven. They display their feeling by staying away [from church]. Turn into the streets [on a Sunday] and mark the rigid gloom that reigns over everything around."[[97]](#cite_note-Callow2012p63-98)[[98]](#cite_note-Dickens1836-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Portrait_of_Charles_Dickens_%284671094%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Charles_Dickens_%284671094%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Charles_Dickens_(4671094).jpg)

Portrait of Dickens, c. 1850, [National Library of Wales](/wiki/National_Library_of_Wales "National Library of Wales")

Dickens honoured the figure of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ").[[99]](#cite_note-100) He is regarded as a professing Christian.[[100]](#cite_note-101) His son, [Henry Fielding Dickens](/wiki/Henry_Fielding_Dickens "Henry Fielding Dickens"), described him as someone who "possessed deep religious convictions". In the early 1840s, he had shown an interest in [Unitarian Christianity](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") and [Robert Browning](/wiki/Robert_Browning "Robert Browning") remarked that "Mr Dickens is an enlightened Unitarian."[[101]](#cite_note-102) Professor Gary Colledge has written that he "never strayed from his attachment to popular lay [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism")".[[102]](#cite_note-103) Dickens authored a work called *[The Life of Our Lord](/wiki/The_Life_of_Our_Lord "The Life of Our Lord")* (1846), a book about the life of Christ, written with the purpose of sharing his faith with his children and family.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) In a scene from *David Copperfield*, Dickens echoed [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")'s use of [Luke 23:34](/wiki/Sayings_of_Jesus_on_the_cross#Luke_23:34 "Sayings of Jesus on the cross") from *[Troilus and Criseyde](/wiki/Troilus_and_Criseyde "Troilus and Criseyde")* (Dickens held a copy in his library), with [G. K. Chesterton](/wiki/G._K._Chesterton "G. K. Chesterton") writing, "among the great [canonical](/wiki/Gospel#Canonical_gospels:_Matthew,_Mark,_Luke_and_John "Gospel") English authors, Chaucer and Dickens have the most in common."[[105]](#cite_note-106)

Dickens disapproved of [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") and 19th-century [evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), seeing both as extremes of Christianity and likely to limit personal expression, and was critical of what he saw as the hypocrisy of religious institutions and philosophies like [spiritualism](/wiki/Spiritualism_(movement) "Spiritualism (movement)"), all of which he considered deviations from the true spirit of Christianity, as shown in the book he wrote for his family in 1846.[[106]](#cite_note-KSmith-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108) While Dickens advocated equal rights for Catholics in England, he strongly disliked how individual civil liberties were often threatened in countries where Catholicism predominated and referred to the Catholic Church as "that curse upon the world."[[106]](#cite_note-KSmith-107) Dickens also rejected the Evangelical conviction that the Bible was the infallible word of God. His ideas on Biblical interpretation were similar to the Liberal Anglican [Arthur Penrhyn Stanley](/wiki/Arthur_Penrhyn_Stanley "Arthur Penrhyn Stanley")'s doctrine of "[progressive revelation](/wiki/Progressive_revelation_(Christianity) "Progressive revelation (Christianity)")".[[106]](#cite_note-KSmith-107) [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy") and [Fyodor Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoyevsky "Fyodor Dostoyevsky") referred to Dickens as "that great Christian writer".[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)

## Middle years

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/David_reaches_Canterbury%2C_from_David_Copperfield_art_by_Frank_Reynolds.jpg/250px-David_reaches_Canterbury%2C_from_David_Copperfield_art_by_Frank_Reynolds.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_reaches_Canterbury,_from_David_Copperfield_art_by_Frank_Reynolds.jpg)

[David](/wiki/David_Copperfield_(character) "David Copperfield (character)") reaches Canterbury, from *David Copperfield*. The character incorporates many elements of Dickens's own life. Artwork by [Frank Reynolds](/wiki/Frank_Reynolds_(artist) "Frank Reynolds (artist)").

In December 1845 Dickens took up the editorship of the London-based *[Daily News](/wiki/The_Daily_News_(UK) "The Daily News (UK)")*, a liberal paper through which Dickens hoped to advocate, in his own words, "the Principles of Progress and Improvement, of Education and Civil and Religious Liberty and Equal Legislation."[[110]](#cite_note-Roberts-111) Among the other contributors Dickens chose to write for the paper were the radical economist [Thomas Hodgskin](/wiki/Thomas_Hodgskin "Thomas Hodgskin") and the social reformer [Douglas William Jerrold](/wiki/Douglas_William_Jerrold "Douglas William Jerrold"), who frequently attacked the [Corn Laws](/wiki/Corn_Laws "Corn Laws").[[110]](#cite_note-Roberts-111)[[111]](#cite_note-112) Dickens lasted only ten weeks on the job before resigning due to a combination of exhaustion and frustration with one of the paper's co-owners.[[110]](#cite_note-Roberts-111)

A [Francophile](/wiki/Francophile "Francophile"), Dickens often holidayed in France and, in a speech delivered in Paris in 1846 in French, called the French "the first people in the universe".[[112]](#cite_note-Soubigou_pages_154-167-113) During his visit to Paris, Dickens met the French literati [Alexandre Dumas](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas "Alexandre Dumas"), [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo"), [Eugène Scribe](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Scribe "Eugène Scribe"), [Théophile Gautier](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophile_Gautier "Théophile Gautier"), [François-René de Chateaubriand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Ren%C3%A9_de_Chateaubriand "François-René de Chateaubriand") and [Eugène Sue](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Sue "Eugène Sue").[[112]](#cite_note-Soubigou_pages_154-167-113) In early 1849 Dickens started to write *[David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield "David Copperfield")*. It was published between 1849 and 1850. In Dickens's biography, *Life of Charles Dickens* (1872), [John Forster](/wiki/John_Forster_(biographer) "John Forster (biographer)") wrote of *David Copperfield*, "underneath the fiction lay something of the author's life".[[113]](#cite_note-114) It was Dickens's personal favourite among his novels, as he wrote in the preface to the 1867 edition.[[114]](#cite_note-115) His [collection of letters](/wiki/Letters_of_Charles_Dickens "Letters of Charles Dickens"), of which more than 14,000 are known, covered a wide range of subject-matter. Letters during this period included a correspondence with Mary Tyler, dated 6 November 1849, on the comedic merits of [Punch and Judy](/wiki/Punch_and_Judy "Punch and Judy"), a puppet show dominated by the anarchic clowning of Mr. Punch, and his review of the [Great Exhibition](/wiki/Great_Exhibition "Great Exhibition"), the first in a series of world's fairs, which he attended at [Hyde Park, London](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London"), in 1851.[[115]](#cite_note-116) After the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") Dickens viewed China through a lens of both mockery and fear, alluding to the Western "yellow peril" stereotype.[[116]](#cite_note-117) During his visit to the Great Exhibition, he scorned Chinese exhibits as symbols of stagnation, labelling them a "glory of yellow jaundice", writing progress was hindered by the isolationist approach of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") with "China, shutting itself up, as far as possible, within itself."[[117]](#cite_note-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Chesney_Wold_%28Frontispiece%29%2C_Bleak_House_%281852-3%29_plate.png/250px-Chesney_Wold_%28Frontispiece%29%2C_Bleak_House_%281852-3%29_plate.png)](/wiki/File:Chesney_Wold_(Frontispiece),_Bleak_House_(1852-3)_plate.png)

Illustration by [Phiz](/wiki/Phiz "Phiz") of Chesney Wold, the Lincolnshire estate in *Bleak House*

In late 1851, Dickens moved into [Tavistock House](/wiki/Tavistock_House "Tavistock House") where he wrote *[Bleak House](/wiki/Bleak_House "Bleak House")* (1852–53), *[Hard Times](/wiki/Hard_Times_(novel) "Hard Times (novel)")* (1854) and *[Little Dorrit](/wiki/Little_Dorrit "Little Dorrit")* (1855–57).[[118]](#cite_note-119) A work incorporating Gothic elements such as the depiction of London as a murky city swathed in [fog](/wiki/Smog "Smog"), *Bleak House* is credited with introducing urban fog to the novel, which would become a frequent characteristic of urban Gothic literature and film.[[119]](#cite_note-120) Reflecting the public enthusiasm for [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") that first developed in Victorian England, the opening of *Bleak House* contains an early mention of dinosaurs in literature: "it would not be wonderful to meet a *[Megalosaurus](/wiki/Megalosaurus "Megalosaurus")*, forty feet long or so, waddling like an elephantine lizard up [Holborn](/wiki/Holborn "Holborn") Hill".[[120]](#cite_note-121)

While at Tavistock Dickens indulged in amateur theatricals, and he worked closely with the novelist and playwright [Wilkie Collins](/wiki/Wilkie_Collins "Wilkie Collins").[[121]](#cite_note-122) In 1856, his income from writing allowed him to buy [Gads Hill Place](/wiki/Gads_Hill_Place "Gads Hill Place") in [Higham, Kent](/wiki/Higham,_Kent "Higham, Kent"). As a child Dickens had walked past the house and dreamt of living in it. The area was also the scene of some of the events of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s *[Henry IV, Part 1](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Part_1 "Henry IV, Part 1")* and this literary connection pleased him.[[122]](#cite_note-123) During this time Dickens was also the publisher, editor and a major contributor to the journals *[Household Words](/wiki/Household_Words "Household Words")* (1850–1859) and *[All the Year Round](/wiki/All_the_Year_Round "All the Year Round")* (1858–1870), with both titles deriving from a Shakespearean quotation.[[123]](#cite_note-124) The journals contained a mix of fiction and non-fiction, and dealt with aspects in the culture. For example, the latter included Dickens's assessment of [Madame Tussauds](/wiki/Madame_Tussauds "Madame Tussauds"), a wax museum established in Baker Street in 1835, which he called "something more than an exhibition, it is an institution."[[124]](#cite_note-125) In 1854, at the behest of [Sir John Franklin](/wiki/Sir_John_Franklin "Sir John Franklin")'s widow [Lady Jane](/wiki/Jane_Franklin "Jane Franklin"), Dickens viciously attacked Arctic explorer [John Rae](/wiki/John_Rae_(explorer) "John Rae (explorer)") in *Household Words* for his report to the [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty (United Kingdom)"), based on interviews with local [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), that the members of [Franklin's lost expedition](/wiki/Franklin%27s_lost_expedition "Franklin's lost expedition") had resorted to [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism"). These attacks would later be expanded on his 1856 play *[The Frozen Deep](/wiki/The_Frozen_Deep "The Frozen Deep")*, a collaboration with Wilkie Collins, which satirises Rae and the Inuit. Twentieth-century [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") work in [King William Island](/wiki/King_William_Island "King William Island") later confirmed that the members of the Franklin expedition resorted to cannibalism.[[125]](#cite_note-Roobol-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Dickens-plaque-tavistock.jpg/250px-Dickens-plaque-tavistock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dickens-plaque-tavistock.jpg)

Commemorative [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") in [Tavistock Square](/wiki/Tavistock_Square "Tavistock Square"), London where Dickens lived between 1851 and 1860

In 1855, when Dickens's good friend the Liberal MP [Austen Henry Layard](/wiki/Austen_Henry_Layard "Austen Henry Layard") formed an Administrative Reform Association to demand significant reforms of Parliament, Dickens joined and volunteered his resources in support of Layard's cause.[[126]](#cite_note-Slater_2009_389–390-127) With the exception of [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell "Lord John Russell"), who was the only leading politician in whom Dickens had any faith and to whom he later dedicated *A Tale of Two Cities*, Dickens believed that the political aristocracy and their incompetence were the death of England.[[126]](#cite_note-Slater_2009_389–390-127)[[127]](#cite_note-Cotsell-128) When he and Layard were accused of fomenting class conflict, Dickens replied that the classes were already in opposition and the fault was with the aristocratic class. Dickens used his pulpit in *Household Words* to champion the Reform Association.[[127]](#cite_note-Cotsell-128) He also commented on foreign affairs, declaring his support for [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") and [Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini"), helping raise funds for their campaigns and stating that "a united Italy would be of vast importance to the peace of the world, and would be a rock in [Louis Napoleon](/wiki/Louis_Napoleon "Louis Napoleon")'s way," and that "I feel for Italy almost as if I were an Italian born."[[128]](#cite_note-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-Ledger-131) Dickens also published dozens of writings in *Household Words* supporting [vaccination](/wiki/Vaccination "Vaccination"), including multiple laudations for the vaccine pioneer [Edward Jenner](/wiki/Edward_Jenner "Edward Jenner").[[131]](#cite_note-132)

Following the [Indian Mutiny of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), Dickens joined in the widespread criticism of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") for its role in the event, but reserved his fury for Indians, wishing that he was the commander-in-chief in India so that he would be able to "do my utmost to exterminate the Race upon whom the stain of the late cruelties rested."[[132]](#cite_note-133)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Ellen_Ternan.jpeg/250px-Ellen_Ternan.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ellen_Ternan.jpeg)

The actress [Ellen Ternan](/wiki/Ellen_Ternan "Ellen Ternan") (pictured in 1858) drew the attention of Dickens after he saw her on stage in 1857.

In 1857 Dickens hired professional actresses for *The Frozen Deep*, which he and his [protégé](/wiki/Mentorship "Mentorship") [Wilkie Collins](/wiki/Wilkie_Collins "Wilkie Collins") had written. Dickens fell in love with one of the actresses, [Ellen Ternan](/wiki/Ellen_Ternan "Ellen Ternan"), and this passion was to last the rest of his life.[[133]](#cite_note-134) In 1858, when Dickens was 45 and Ternan 18, divorce would have been scandalous for someone of his fame. After publicly accusing Catherine of not loving their children and suffering from "a mental disorder"—statements that disgusted his contemporaries, including [Elizabeth Barrett Browning](/wiki/Elizabeth_Barrett_Browning "Elizabeth Barrett Browning")[[134]](#cite_note-135)—Dickens attempted to have Catherine [institutionalised](/wiki/Lunatic_asylum "Lunatic asylum").[[135]](#cite_note-136) When his scheme failed, they separated. Catherine left, never to see her husband again, taking with her one child. Her sister Georgina, who stayed at Gads Hill, raised the other children.[[79]](#cite_note-Smith10ff-80)

During this period, whilst pondering a project to give public readings for his own profit, Dickens was approached through a charitable appeal by [Great Ormond Street Hospital](/wiki/Great_Ormond_Street_Hospital "Great Ormond Street Hospital") to help it survive its first major financial crisis. His "Drooping Buds" essay in *[Household Words](/wiki/Household_Words "Household Words")* earlier on 3 April 1852 was considered by the hospital's founders to have been the catalyst for the hospital's success.[[136]](#cite_note-137) Dickens, whose philanthropy was well-known, was asked by his friend, the hospital's founder [Charles West](/wiki/Charles_West_(physician) "Charles West (physician)"), to preside over the appeal, and he threw himself into the task, heart and soul.[[137]](#cite_note-138) Dickens's public readings secured sufficient funds for an endowment to put the hospital on a sound financial footing; one reading on 9 February 1858 alone raised £3,000.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Dickens_by_Watkins_1858.png/250px-Dickens_by_Watkins_1858.png)](/wiki/File:Dickens_by_Watkins_1858.png)

Dickens at his desk, 1858, by [Herbert Watkins](/wiki/Herbert_Watkins "Herbert Watkins")

After separating from Catherine,[[141]](#cite_note-142) Dickens undertook a series of popular and remunerative reading tours which, together with his journalism, were to absorb most of his creative energies for the next decade, in which he was to write only two novels.[[142]](#cite_note-143) His first reading tour, lasting from April 1858 to February 1859, consisted of 129 appearances in 49 towns throughout England, Scotland and Ireland.[[143]](#cite_note-144) Dickens's continued fascination with the theatrical world was written into the theatre scenes in *Nicholas Nickleby*, and he found an outlet in public readings. In 1866, he undertook a series of public readings in England and Scotland, with more the following year in England and Ireland.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Ye_Olde_Cheshire_Cheese%2C_Fleet_Street%2C_EC4_%288032557646%29.jpg/250px-Ye_Olde_Cheshire_Cheese%2C_Fleet_Street%2C_EC4_%288032557646%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ye_Olde_Cheshire_Cheese,_Fleet_Street,_EC4_(8032557646).jpg)

Dickens was a regular patron at [Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese](/wiki/Ye_Olde_Cheshire_Cheese "Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese") pub in [Fleet Street](/wiki/Fleet_Street "Fleet Street"), London. He included the venue in *A Tale of Two Cities*.

Other works soon followed, including *[A Tale of Two Cities](/wiki/A_Tale_of_Two_Cities "A Tale of Two Cities")* (1859) and *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations "Great Expectations")* (1861), which were resounding successes. Set in London and Paris, *A Tale of Two Cities* is his best-known work of historical fiction and includes the famous opening sentence "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times." It is regularly touted as one of the best-selling novels of all time.[[145]](#cite_note-146)[[146]](#cite_note-147) Themes in *Great Expectations* include wealth and poverty, love and rejection, and the eventual triumph of good over evil.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

In early September 1860, in a field behind Gads Hill, Dickens made a bonfire of most of his correspondence; he spared only letters on business matters. Since Ellen Ternan also destroyed all of his letters to her,[[148]](#cite_note-149) the extent of the affair between the two remains speculative.[[149]](#cite_note-150) In the 1930s, Thomas Wright recounted that Ternan had unburdened herself to a Canon Benham and gave currency to rumours they had been lovers.[[150]](#cite_note-151) Dickens's daughter, Kate Perugini, stated that the two had a son who died in infancy to biographer Gladys Storey in an interview before the former's death in 1929. Storey published her account in *Dickens and Daughter*,[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153) though no contemporary evidence was given. On his death, Dickens settled an [annuity](/wiki/Life_annuity "Life annuity") on Ternan which made her financially independent. [Claire Tomalin](/wiki/Claire_Tomalin "Claire Tomalin")'s book *The Invisible Woman* argues that Ternan lived with Dickens secretly for the last 13 years of his life. The book was turned into a play, *Little Nell*, by [Simon Gray](/wiki/Simon_Gray "Simon Gray"), and [a 2013 film](/wiki/The_Invisible_Woman_(2013_film) "The Invisible Woman (2013 film)"). During the same period, Dickens furthered his interest in the [paranormal](/wiki/Paranormal "Paranormal"), becoming one of the early members of [The Ghost Club](/wiki/The_Ghost_Club "The Ghost Club") in London.[[153]](#cite_note-154) In Christmas Eve of 1862, a theatrical production of his novella, *[The Haunted Man and the Ghost's Bargain](/wiki/The_Haunted_Man_and_the_Ghost%27s_Bargain "The Haunted Man and the Ghost's Bargain")*, saw the first public demonstration of "[Pepper's ghost](/wiki/Pepper%27s_ghost "Pepper's ghost")"—a method of projecting the illusion of a ghost into a theatre (named after its developer [John Henry Pepper](/wiki/John_Henry_Pepper "John Henry Pepper"))—which caused a sensation among those in attendance at the [Regent Street](/wiki/Regent_Street "Regent Street") theatre.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

In June 1862 he was offered £10,000 for a reading tour of Australia.[[155]](#cite_note-156) He was enthusiastic, and even planned a travel book, *The Uncommercial Traveller Upside Down*, but ultimately decided against the tour.[[156]](#cite_note-157) Two of his sons, [Alfred D'Orsay Tennyson Dickens](/wiki/Alfred_D%27Orsay_Tennyson_Dickens "Alfred D'Orsay Tennyson Dickens") and [Edward Bulwer Lytton Dickens](/wiki/Edward_Dickens "Edward Dickens"), migrated to Australia, Edward becoming a member of the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales") as [Member for Wilcannia](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Wilcannia "Electoral district of Wilcannia") between 1889 and 1894.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)

## Later life

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Staplehurst_rail_crash.jpg/250px-Staplehurst_rail_crash.jpg)](/wiki/File:Staplehurst_rail_crash.jpg)

Aftermath of the [Staplehurst rail crash](/wiki/Staplehurst_rail_crash "Staplehurst rail crash") in 1865

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Charles_Dickens_E_Edwards_1864.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens_E_Edwards_1864.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens_E_Edwards_1864.jpg)

Dickens, c. 1866, by Ernest Edwards

On 9 June 1865, while returning from Paris with Ellen Ternan, Dickens was involved in the [Staplehurst rail crash](/wiki/Staplehurst_rail_crash "Staplehurst rail crash") in Kent. The train's first seven carriages plunged off a [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") bridge that was under repair and ten passengers were killed.[[159]](#cite_note-160) The only [first-class](/wiki/First_class_travel "First class travel") carriage to remain on the track—which was left hanging precariously off the bridge—was the one in which Dickens was travelling.[[160]](#cite_note-Grass-161) For three hours before rescuers arrived, Dickens tended and comforted the wounded and the dying with a flask of brandy and a hat refreshed with water.[[160]](#cite_note-Grass-161) Before leaving, he remembered the unfinished manuscript for *[Our Mutual Friend](/wiki/Our_Mutual_Friend "Our Mutual Friend")*, and he returned to his carriage to retrieve it.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

Dickens later used the experience of the crash as material for his short [ghost story](/wiki/Ghost_story "Ghost story"), "[The Signal-Man](/wiki/The_Signal-Man "The Signal-Man")", in which the central character has a premonition of his own death in a rail crash. He also based the story on several previous [rail accidents](/wiki/Lists_of_rail_accidents "Lists of rail accidents"), such as the [Clayton Tunnel rail crash](/wiki/Clayton_Tunnel_rail_crash "Clayton Tunnel rail crash") in Sussex of 1861. Dickens managed to avoid an appearance at the [inquest](/wiki/Inquest "Inquest") to avoid disclosing that he had been travelling with Ternan and her mother, which would have caused a scandal.[[162]](#cite_note-163) After the crash, Dickens was nervous when travelling by train and would use alternative means when available.[[163]](#cite_note-UOC-164) In 1868 he wrote, "I have sudden vague rushes of terror, even when riding in a hansom cab, which are perfectly unreasonable but quite insurmountable." Dickens's son, Henry, recalled, "I have seen him sometimes in a railway carriage when there was a slight jolt. When this happened he was almost in a state of panic and gripped the seat with both hands."[[163]](#cite_note-UOC-164)

### Second visit to the United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Buying_tickets_for_a_Charles_Dickens_reading_at_Steinway_Hall%2C_New_York%2C_New_York%2C_1867.jpg/250px-Buying_tickets_for_a_Charles_Dickens_reading_at_Steinway_Hall%2C_New_York%2C_New_York%2C_1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buying_tickets_for_a_Charles_Dickens_reading_at_Steinway_Hall,_New_York,_New_York,_1867.jpg)

Crowd of spectators buying tickets for a Dickens reading at [Steinway Hall](/wiki/Steinway_Hall "Steinway Hall"), New York City, in 1867

While he contemplated a second visit to the United States, the outbreak of the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") in America in 1861 delayed his plans.[[164]](#cite_note-165) On 9 November 1867, over two years after the war, Dickens set sail from [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") for his second American reading tour. Landing in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), he devoted the rest of the month to a round of dinners with such notables as [Ralph Waldo Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson"), [Henry Wadsworth Longfellow](/wiki/Henry_Wadsworth_Longfellow "Henry Wadsworth Longfellow") and his American publisher, [James T. Fields](/wiki/James_T._Fields "James T. Fields"). In early December, the readings began. He performed 76 readings, netting £19,000, from December 1867 to April 1868.[[165]](#cite_note-Hobsbaum1998-166) Dickens shuttled between Boston and New York, where he gave 22 readings at [Steinway Hall](/wiki/Steinway_Hall "Steinway Hall"). Although he had started to suffer from what he called the "true American [catarrh](/wiki/Catarrh "Catarrh")", he kept to a packed schedule, even managing to squeeze in some sleighing in [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park").[[166]](#cite_note-167)

During his travels, he saw a change in the people and the circumstances of America. His final appearance was at a banquet the American Press held in his honour at [Delmonico's](/wiki/Delmonico%27s_Restaurant "Delmonico's Restaurant") on 18 April, when he promised never to denounce America again. By the end of the tour Dickens could hardly manage solid food, subsisting on champagne and eggs beaten in sherry. On 23 April he boarded the [Cunard](/wiki/Cunard "Cunard") liner [*Russia*](/wiki/SS_Waesland "SS Waesland") to return to Britain,[[167]](#cite_note-168) barely escaping a [federal tax lien](/wiki/Tax_lien "Tax lien") against the proceeds of his lecture tour.[[168]](#cite_note-169)

### Farewell readings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Dickensposter_nottingham1869.jpg/250px-Dickensposter_nottingham1869.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dickensposter_nottingham1869.jpg)

Poster promoting a reading by Dickens in [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham") dated 4 February 1869, two months before he had a mild stroke

In 1868–69 Dickens gave a series of "farewell readings" in England, Scotland and Ireland, beginning on 6 October. He managed, of a contracted 100 readings, to give 75 in the provinces, with a further 12 in London.[[165]](#cite_note-Hobsbaum1998-166) As he pressed on he was affected by giddiness and fits of paralysis. He had a stroke on 18 April 1869 in [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester").[[169]](#cite_note-Tomalin2011p377-170) He collapsed on 22 April 1869, in [Preston, Lancashire](/wiki/Preston,_Lancashire "Preston, Lancashire"); on doctor's advice, the tour was cancelled.[[170]](#cite_note-171) After further provincial readings were cancelled, he began work on his final novel, *[The Mystery of Edwin Drood](/wiki/The_Mystery_of_Edwin_Drood "The Mystery of Edwin Drood")*. Described as a "dark and gothic" tale, his unfinished novel focuses on Drood's uncle, John Jasper, a drug-addicted choirmaster.[[171]](#cite_note-172) It was fashionable in the 1860s to 'do the slums' and, in company, Dickens visited [opium dens](/wiki/Opium_den "Opium den") in [Shadwell](/wiki/Shadwell "Shadwell") in the East End of London, where he witnessed an elderly addict called "[Laskar](/wiki/Lascar "Lascar") Sal", who formed the model for "Opium Sal" in *Edwin Drood*.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

After Dickens regained enough strength, he arranged, with medical approval, for a final series of readings to partly make up to his sponsors what they had lost due to his illness. There were 12 performances, on 11 January to 15 March 1870; the last at 8:00pm at [St James's Hall](/wiki/St_James%27s_Hall "St James's Hall"), London. Though in grave health by then, he read *A Christmas Carol* and *The Trial from Pickwick*. On 2 May, he made his last public appearance at a [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts") banquet in the presence of the [Prince](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") and [Princess of Wales](/wiki/Alexandra_of_Denmark "Alexandra of Denmark"), paying a special tribute on the death of his friend the illustrator Daniel Maclise.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

### Death

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Samuel_Luke_Fildes_-_The_Empty_Chair_%28The_Graphic%2C_1870%29.jpg/250px-Samuel_Luke_Fildes_-_The_Empty_Chair_%28The_Graphic%2C_1870%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samuel_Luke_Fildes_-_The_Empty_Chair_(The_Graphic,_1870).jpg)

[Samuel Luke Fildes](/wiki/Luke_Fildes "Luke Fildes") *– The Empty Chair*. Fildes was illustrating *Edwin Drood* at the time of Dickens's death. The engraving shows Dickens's empty chair in his study at [Gads Hill Place](/wiki/Gads_Hill_Place "Gads Hill Place"). It appeared in the Christmas 1870 edition of *[The Graphic](/wiki/The_Graphic "The Graphic")* and thousands of prints of it were sold.[[174]](#cite_note-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Charles_Dickens_grave_2012.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens_grave_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens_grave_2012.jpg)

Dickens's grave in [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Charles_Dickens_Death_Certificate.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens_Death_Certificate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens_Death_Certificate.jpg)

A 1905 transcribed copy of the death certificate of Charles Dickens

On 8 June 1870 Dickens had another stroke at his home after a full day's work on *Edwin Drood*. He never regained consciousness. The next day, he died at Gads Hill Place, aged 58. His biographer Claire Tomalin has suggested Dickens was actually in Peckham when he had had the stroke and his mistress Ellen Ternan and her maids had him taken back to Gads Hill so that the public would not know the truth about their relationship.[[175]](#cite_note-Tomalin2011p395-176) Contrary to his wish to be buried at [Rochester Cathedral](/wiki/Rochester_Cathedral "Rochester Cathedral") "in an inexpensive, unostentatious, and strictly private manner",[[176]](#cite_note-177) he was laid to rest in [Poets' Corner](/wiki/Poets%27_Corner "Poets' Corner") in [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"). A printed epitaph circulated at the time of the funeral reads:

> To the Memory of Charles Dickens (England's most popular author) who died at his residence, Higham, near Rochester, Kent, 9 June 1870, aged 58 years. He was a sympathiser with the poor, the suffering, and the oppressed; and by his death, one of England's greatest writers is lost to the world.[[177]](#cite_note-178)

A letter from Dickens to the Clerk of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council") in March indicates he had been offered and accepted a [baronetcy](/wiki/Baronetcy "Baronetcy"), which was not gazetted before his death.[[178]](#cite_note-179) His last words were "On the ground" in response to his sister-in-law Georgina's request that he lie down.[[179]](#cite_note-180)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-181) On Sunday, 19 June 1870, five days after Dickens was buried in the Abbey, Dean [Arthur Penrhyn Stanley](/wiki/Arthur_Penrhyn_Stanley "Arthur Penrhyn Stanley") delivered a memorial elegy, lauding "the genial and loving humorist whom we now mourn", for showing by his own example "that even in dealing with the darkest scenes and the most degraded characters, genius could still be clean, and mirth could be innocent". Pointing to the fresh flowers that adorned the novelist's grave, Stanley assured those present that "the spot would thenceforth be a sacred one with both the New World and the Old, as that of the representative of literature, not of this island only, but of all who speak our English tongue."[[180]](#cite_note-182)

In his will, drafted more than a year before his death, Dickens left the care of his £80,000 estate (£8,000,000 in 2023)[[181]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-183) to his long-time colleague John Forster and his "best and truest friend" Georgina Hogarth who, along with Dickens's two sons, also received a tax-free sum of £8,000 (equivalent to £800,000 in 2023).[[181]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-183) He confirmed his wife Catherine's annual allowance
