of £600 (£60,000 in 2023).[[181]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-183) He bequeathed £19 19s (£2,000 in 2023)[[181]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-183) to each servant in his employment at the time of his death.[[182]](#cite_note-184)

## Literary style

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/The_Night.jpg/250px-The_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Night.jpg)

"The Night" scene in *Bleak House*, depicting a murky [Westminster Bridge](/wiki/Westminster_Bridge "Westminster Bridge") in London. Dickens set the Gothic novel in an urban environment.

Dickens's approach to the novel is influenced by various things, including the [picaresque novel](/wiki/Picaresque_novel "Picaresque novel") tradition,[[183]](#cite_note-Levin1970p676-185) [melodrama](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama")[[184]](#cite_note-Levin1970p674-186) and the [novel of sensibility](/wiki/Novel_of_sensibility "Novel of sensibility").[[185]](#cite_note-Purton2012pxvii-187) According to Ackroyd, other than these, perhaps the most important literary influence on him was derived from the fables of *[The Arabian Nights](/wiki/The_Arabian_Nights "The Arabian Nights")*.[[186]](#cite_note-188) Satire and [irony](/wiki/Irony "Irony") are central to the picaresque novel.[[187]](#cite_note-189) Comedy is also an aspect of the British picaresque novel tradition of [Laurence Sterne](/wiki/Laurence_Sterne "Laurence Sterne"), [Henry Fielding](/wiki/Henry_Fielding "Henry Fielding") and [Tobias Smollett](/wiki/Tobias_Smollett "Tobias Smollett"). Fielding's *[Tom Jones](/wiki/The_History_of_Tom_Jones,_a_Foundling "The History of Tom Jones, a Foundling")* was a major influence on the 19th-century novelist including Dickens, who read it in his youth[[188]](#cite_note-190) and named a son [Henry Fielding Dickens](/wiki/Henry_Fielding_Dickens "Henry Fielding Dickens") after him.[[189]](#cite_note-HFDickens1934pxviii-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192) Influenced by [Gothic fiction](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction")—a literary genre that began with *[The Castle of Otranto](/wiki/The_Castle_of_Otranto "The Castle of Otranto")* (1764) by [Horace Walpole](/wiki/Horace_Walpole "Horace Walpole")—Dickens incorporated Gothic imagery, settings and plot devices in his works.[[191]](#cite_note-193) Victorian gothic moved from castles and abbeys into contemporary urban environments: in particular London, such as Dickens's *Oliver Twist* and *Bleak House*. The jilted bride [Miss Havisham](/wiki/Miss_Havisham "Miss Havisham") from *Great Expectations* is one of Dickens's best-known gothic creations; living in a ruined mansion, her bridal gown effectively doubles as her funeral shroud.[[192]](#cite_note-194)

No other writer had such a profound influence on Dickens as [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"). On Dickens's veneration of Shakespeare, [Alfred Harbage](/wiki/Alfred_Harbage "Alfred Harbage") wrote in *A Kind of Power: The Shakespeare-Dickens Analogy* (1975) that "No one is better qualified to recognise literary genius than a literary genius".[[193]](#cite_note-Schlicke-195) Regarding Shakespeare as "the great master" whose [plays](/wiki/Shakespeare%27s_plays "Shakespeare's plays") "were an unspeakable source of delight", Dickens's lifelong affinity with the playwright included seeing theatrical productions of his plays in London and putting on amateur dramatics with friends in his early years.[[193]](#cite_note-Schlicke-195) In 1838, Dickens travelled to [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon") and visited the house in which Shakespeare was born, leaving his autograph in the visitors' book. Dickens would draw on this experience in his next work, *Nicholas Nickleby* (1838–39), expressing the strength of feeling experienced by visitors to Shakespeare's birthplace: the character [Mrs Wititterly](/wiki/Nicholas_Nickleby#Around_London "Nicholas Nickleby") states, "I don't know how it is, but after you've seen the place and written your name in the little book, somehow or other you seem to be inspired; it kindles up quite a fire within one."[[194]](#cite_note-196)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Clarke-dodger.jpg/250px-Clarke-dodger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clarke-dodger.jpg)

The [Artful Dodger](/wiki/Artful_Dodger "Artful Dodger") from *Oliver Twist*. His dialect is rooted in [Cockney English](/wiki/Cockney_English "Cockney English").

Dickens's writing style is marked by a profuse linguistic creativity.[[195]](#cite_note-Mee2010-197) Satire, flourishing in his gift for caricature, is his forte. An early reviewer compared him to the artist and social critic [Hogarth](/wiki/William_Hogarth "William Hogarth") for his keen practical sense of the ludicrous side of life, though his acclaimed mastery of varieties of class idiom may in fact mirror the conventions of contemporary popular theatre.[[196]](#cite_note-198) Dickens worked intensively on developing arresting names for his characters that would reverberate with associations for his readers and assist the development of motifs in the storyline, giving what one critic calls an "allegorical impetus" to the novels' meanings.[[195]](#cite_note-Mee2010-197) To cite one of numerous examples, the name Mr Murdstone in *David Copperfield* conjures up twin allusions to murder and stony coldness.[[197]](#cite_note-199) His literary style is also a mixture of fantasy and [realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)"). His satires of British aristocratic snobbery—he calls one character the "Noble Refrigerator"—are often popular. Comparing orphans to stocks and shares, people to tug boats or dinner-party guests to furniture are just some of Dickens's acclaimed flights of fancy. On his ability to elicit a response from his works, the English screenwriter [Sarah Phelps](/wiki/Sarah_Phelps "Sarah Phelps") writes, "He knew how to work an audience and how to get them laughing their heads off one minute or on the edge of their seats and holding their breath the next. The other thing about Dickens is that he loved telling stories and he loved his characters, even those horrible, mean-spirited ones."[[198]](#cite_note-200)

The author worked closely with his illustrators, supplying them with a summary of the work at the outset and thus ensuring that his characters and settings were exactly how he envisioned them. He briefed the illustrator on plans for each month's instalment so that work could begin before he wrote them. [Marcus Stone](/wiki/Marcus_Stone "Marcus Stone"), illustrator of *Our Mutual Friend*, recalled that the author was always "ready to describe down to the minutest details the personal characteristics, and ... life-history of the creations of his fancy".[[199]](#cite_note-201) Dickens employs [Cockney English](/wiki/Cockney_English "Cockney English") in many of his works, denoting working-class Londoners. Cockney grammar appears in terms such as [ain't](/wiki/Ain%27t "Ain't"), and consonants in words are frequently omitted, as in 'ere (here) and wot (what).[[200]](#cite_note-202) An example of this usage is in *Oliver Twist*. The Artful Dodger uses cockney slang which is juxtaposed with Oliver's 'proper' English, when the Dodger repeats Oliver saying "seven" with "sivin".[[201]](#cite_note-203)

### Characters

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Dickens_dream.jpg/250px-Dickens_dream.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dickens_dream.jpg)

*Dickens's Dream* by [Robert William Buss](/wiki/Robert_William_Buss "Robert William Buss"), portraying Dickens at his desk at [Gads Hill Place](/wiki/Gads_Hill_Place "Gads Hill Place") surrounded by many of his characters

Dickens's biographer [Claire Tomalin](/wiki/Claire_Tomalin "Claire Tomalin") regards him as the greatest creator of character in English fiction after Shakespeare.[[202]](#cite_note-204) Dickensian [characters](/wiki/List_of_Dickensian_characters "List of Dickensian characters") are amongst the most memorable in English literature, especially so because of their typically whimsical names. The likes of [Ebenezer Scrooge](/wiki/Ebenezer_Scrooge "Ebenezer Scrooge"), [Tiny Tim](/wiki/Tiny_Tim_(A_Christmas_Carol) "Tiny Tim (A Christmas Carol)"), [Jacob Marley](/wiki/Jacob_Marley "Jacob Marley") and [Bob Cratchit](/wiki/Bob_Cratchit "Bob Cratchit") (*A Christmas Carol*); [Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist_(character) "Oliver Twist (character)"), [The Artful Dodger](/wiki/Artful_Dodger "Artful Dodger"), [Fagin](/wiki/Fagin "Fagin") and [Bill Sikes](/wiki/Bill_Sikes "Bill Sikes") (*Oliver Twist*); [Pip](/wiki/Pip_(Great_Expectations) "Pip (Great Expectations)"), [Miss Havisham](/wiki/Miss_Havisham "Miss Havisham"), [Estella](/wiki/Estella_(Great_Expectations) "Estella (Great Expectations)") and [Abel Magwitch](/wiki/Abel_Magwitch "Abel Magwitch") (*Great Expectations*); [Sydney Carton](/wiki/Sydney_Carton "Sydney Carton"), [Charles Darnay](/wiki/Charles_Darnay "Charles Darnay") and [Madame Defarge](/wiki/Madame_Defarge "Madame Defarge") (*A Tale of Two Cities*); [David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield_(character) "David Copperfield (character)"), [Uriah Heep](/wiki/Uriah_Heep_(character) "Uriah Heep (character)") and [Mr Micawber](/wiki/Wilkins_Micawber "Wilkins Micawber") (*David Copperfield*); [Daniel Quilp](/wiki/Quilp "Quilp") and [Nell Trent](/wiki/Nell_Trent "Nell Trent") (*The Old Curiosity Shop*), [Samuel Pickwick](/wiki/Samuel_Pickwick "Samuel Pickwick") and [Sam Weller](/wiki/Sam_Weller_(character) "Sam Weller (character)") (*The Pickwick Papers*); and [Wackford Squeers](/wiki/Wackford_Squeers "Wackford Squeers") (*Nicholas Nickleby*) are so well known as to be part and parcel of popular culture, and in some cases have passed into ordinary language: a *scrooge*, for example, is a miser or someone who dislikes Christmas festivity.[[203]](#cite_note-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/P_060--In_Dickens_London.jpg/250px-P_060--In_Dickens_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:P_060--In_Dickens_London.jpg)

Illustration of [London Bridge](/wiki/London_Bridge "London Bridge") (from the 1914 book *In Dickens's London*) which [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_(Oliver_Twist) "Nancy (Oliver Twist)") crossed in *Oliver Twist*

His characters were often so memorable that they took on a life of their own outside his books. "Gamp" became a slang expression for an unwieldy [umbrella](/wiki/Umbrella "Umbrella") from the character [Mrs Gamp](/wiki/Sarah_Gamp "Sarah Gamp") because she always carries one, and "Pickwickian", "Pecksniffian" and "Gradgrind" all entered dictionaries due to Dickens's original portraits of such characters who were, respectively, [quixotic](/wiki/Quixotism "Quixotism"), hypocritical and vapidly factual.[[204]](#cite_note-206) Sam Weller, the character that made Dickens famous, became known for his [Wellerisms](/wiki/Wellerism "Wellerism")—one-liners that turn [proverbs](/wiki/Proverb "Proverb") on their heads.[[53]](#cite_note-Paris_Review-54) Many were drawn from real life: Mrs Nickleby is based on his mother, although she did not recognise herself in the portrait,[[205]](#cite_note-207) just as Mr Micawber is constructed from aspects of his father's 'rhetorical exuberance';[[206]](#cite_note-208) Harold Skimpole in *Bleak House* is based on [James Henry Leigh Hunt](/wiki/James_Henry_Leigh_Hunt "James Henry Leigh Hunt"); his wife's dwarfish chiropodist recognised herself in Miss Mowcher in *David Copperfield*.[[207]](#cite_note-209) Perhaps Dickens's impressions on his meeting with [Hans Christian Andersen](/wiki/Hans_Christian_Andersen "Hans Christian Andersen") informed the delineation of Uriah Heep (a term synonymous with [sycophant](/wiki/Sycophant "Sycophant")).[[208]](#cite_note-210)

[Virginia Woolf](/wiki/Virginia_Woolf "Virginia Woolf") maintained that "we remodel our psychological geography when we read Dickens" as he produces "characters who exist not in detail, not accurately or exactly, but abundantly in a cluster of wild yet extraordinarily revealing remarks".[[209]](#cite_note-211) [T. S. Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot") wrote that Dickens "excelled in character; in the creation of characters of greater intensity than human beings".[[210]](#cite_note-212) One "character" vividly drawn throughout his novels is London itself.[[211]](#cite_note-213) Dickens described London as a [magic lantern](/wiki/Magic_lantern "Magic lantern"), inspiring the places and people in many of his novels.[[212]](#cite_note-DickensLondon-214) From the [coaching inns](/wiki/Coaching_inn "Coaching inn") on the outskirts of the city to the lower reaches of the [Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"), all aspects of the capital—[Dickens's London](/wiki/Dickens%27_London "Dickens' London")—are described over the course of his body of work.[[212]](#cite_note-DickensLondon-214) Walking the streets (particularly around London) formed an integral part of his writing life, stoking his creativity. Dickens was known to regularly walk at least a dozen miles (19 km) per day, and once wrote, "If I couldn't walk fast and far, I should just explode and perish."[[213]](#cite_note-215)

### Autobiographical elements

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/David_Copperfield%2C_We_are_disturbed_in_our_cookery.jpg/250px-David_Copperfield%2C_We_are_disturbed_in_our_cookery.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Copperfield,_We_are_disturbed_in_our_cookery.jpg)

An original illustration by [Phiz](/wiki/Phiz "Phiz") from the novel *David Copperfield*, which is widely regarded as Dickens's most autobiographical work

Authors frequently draw their portraits of characters from people they have known in real life. *David Copperfield* is regarded by many as a veiled autobiography of Dickens. The scenes of interminable court cases and legal arguments in *Bleak House* reflect Dickens's experiences as a law clerk and court reporter, and in particular his direct experience of the law's procedural delay during 1844 when he sued publishers in Chancery for breach of copyright.[[214]](#cite_note-216) Dickens's father was sent to prison for debt, and this became a common theme in many of his books, with the detailed depiction of life in the [Marshalsea](/wiki/Marshalsea "Marshalsea") prison in *Little Dorrit* resulting from Dickens's own experiences of the institution.[[215]](#cite_note-217) Lucy Stroughill, a childhood sweetheart, may have affected several of Dickens's portraits of girls such as Little Em'ly in *David Copperfield* and Lucie Manette in *A Tale of Two Cities*.[[216]](#cite_note-218)[[nb 3]](#cite_note-219)

Dickens may have drawn on his childhood experiences, but he was also ashamed of them and would not reveal that this was where he gathered his realistic accounts of squalor. Very few knew the details of his early life until six years after his death, when John Forster published a biography on which Dickens had collaborated. Though Skimpole brutally sends up [Leigh Hunt](/wiki/Leigh_Hunt "Leigh Hunt"), some critics have detected in his portrait features of Dickens's own character, which he sought to exorcise by self-parody.[[217]](#cite_note-220)

### Episodic writing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Publicit%C3%A9_pour_Great_Expectations_dans_All_the_Year_Round.jpeg/250px-Publicit%C3%A9_pour_Great_Expectations_dans_All_the_Year_Round.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Publicit%C3%A9_pour_Great_Expectations_dans_All_the_Year_Round.jpeg)

Advertisement for *Great Expectations*, serialised in the weekly literary magazine *[All the Year Round](/wiki/All_the_Year_Round "All the Year Round")* from December 1860 to August 1861. The advert contains the plot device "to be continued".

A pioneer of the [serial](/wiki/Serial_(literature) "Serial (literature)") publication of narrative fiction, Dickens wrote most of his major novels in monthly or weekly instalments in journals such as *[Master Humphrey's Clock](/wiki/Master_Humphrey%27s_Clock "Master Humphrey's Clock")* and *[Household Words](/wiki/Household_Words "Household Words")*, later reprinted in book form.[[4]](#cite_note-Grossman_2012_54-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Lodge_2002_118-5) These instalments made the stories affordable and accessible, with the audience more evenly distributed across income levels than before.[[218]](#cite_note-Howsam-221) His instalment format inspired a narrative that he would explore and develop throughout his career, and the regular [cliffhangers](/wiki/Cliffhanger "Cliffhanger") made each new episode widely anticipated.[[6]](#cite_note-NewYorker-6)[[218]](#cite_note-Howsam-221) When *[The Old Curiosity Shop](/wiki/The_Old_Curiosity_Shop "The Old Curiosity Shop")* was being serialised, American fans waited at the docks in [New York harbour](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), shouting out to the crew of an incoming British ship, "Is little Nell dead?"[[219]](#cite_note-222) Dickens was able to incorporate this episodic writing style but still end up with a coherent novel at the end. He wrote, "The thing has to be planned for presentation in these fragments, and yet for afterwards fusing together as an uninterrupted whole."[[220]](#cite_note-223)

Another important impact of Dickens's episodic writing style resulted from his exposure to the opinions of his readers and friends. His friend Forster had a significant hand in reviewing his drafts, an influence that went beyond matters of punctuation; he toned down melodramatic and sensationalist exaggerations, cut long passages (such as the episode of Quilp's drowning in *The Old Curiosity Shop*), and made suggestions about plot and character. It was he who suggested that Charley Bates should be redeemed in *Oliver Twist*. Dickens had not thought of killing Little Nell and it was Forster who advised him to entertain this possibility as necessary to his conception of the heroine.[[221]](#cite_note-224) When in 1863 the Jewish English reader [Eliza Davis](/wiki/Eliza_Davis_(letter_writer) "Eliza Davis (letter writer)") wrote to rebuke him for having "encouraged a vile prejudice against the despised Hebrew" with the character of Fagin in *Oliver Twist*, Dickens halted the second printing of the novel and made some changes to the original 1837 text.[[222]](#cite_note-225) He also created a group of sympathetic Jewish characters in his next novel, *[Our Mutual Friend](/wiki/Our_Mutual_Friend "Our Mutual Friend")*, published 1864–1865.[[223]](#cite_note-226)

At the helm in popularising cliffhangers and serial publications in Victorian literature,[[224]](#cite_note-227) Dickens's influence can also be seen in television [soap operas](/wiki/Soap_operas "Soap operas") and [film series](/wiki/Film_series "Film series"), with *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* stating that "the DNA of Dickens's busy, episodic storytelling, delivered in instalments and rife with cliffhangers and diversions, is traceable in everything."[[225]](#cite_note-228) His serialisation of his novels also drew comments from other writers. In the Scottish author [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson")'s novel *[The Wrecker](/wiki/The_Wrecker_(Stevenson_novel) "The Wrecker (Stevenson novel)")*, Captain Nares, investigating an abandoned ship, remarked: "See! They were writing up the log," said Nares, pointing to the ink-bottle. "Caught napping, as usual. I wonder if there ever was a captain yet that lost a ship with his log-book up to date? He generally has about a month to fill up on a clean break, like Charles Dickens and his serial novels."[[226]](#cite_note-229)

### Social commentary

"Dickensian" redirects here. For the television series, see [*Dickensian* (TV series)](/wiki/Dickensian_(TV_series) "Dickensian (TV series)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Martin_Chuzzlewit_illus11.jpg/250px-Martin_Chuzzlewit_illus11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Chuzzlewit_illus11.jpg)

[Sarah Gamp](/wiki/Sarah_Gamp "Sarah Gamp") (left) from *Martin Chuzzlewit* became a stereotype of untrained and incompetent nurses of the early Victorian era, before the reforms of [Florence Nightingale](/wiki/Florence_Nightingale "Florence Nightingale").

Dickens's novels were, among other things, works of [social commentary](/wiki/Social_commentary "Social commentary"). [Simon Callow](/wiki/Simon_Callow "Simon Callow") states, "From the moment he started to write, he spoke for the people, and the people loved him for it."[[227]](#cite_note-230) He was a fierce critic of the poverty and [social stratification](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification") of [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") society. In a New York address, he expressed his belief that "Virtue shows quite as well in rags and patches as she does in purple and fine linen".[[228]](#cite_note-231) Dickens's second novel, *Oliver Twist* (1839), shocked readers with its images of poverty and crime: it challenged middle class polemics about criminals, making impossible any pretence to ignorance about what poverty entailed.[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[230]](#cite_note-233) Today, *Dickensian* is a term applied to insanitary social conditions or grim institutions akin to those denounced by Dickens in his work, with the Oxford professor [Peter Conrad](/wiki/Peter_Conrad_(academic) "Peter Conrad (academic)") writing, "Dickens, like [Banksy](/wiki/Banksy "Banksy"), writes blackly prophetic graffiti on the wall."[[231]](#cite_note-234)

At a time when Britain was the major economic and political power of the world, Dickens highlighted the life of the forgotten poor and disadvantaged within society. Through his journalism he campaigned on specific issues—such as [sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation") and the [workhouse](/wiki/Workhouse "Workhouse")—but his fiction probably demonstrated its greatest prowess in changing public opinion in regard to class inequalities. He often depicted the exploitation and oppression of the poor and condemned the public officials and institutions that not only allowed such abuses to exist, but flourished as a result. His most strident indictment of this condition is in *Hard Times* (1854), Dickens's only novel-length treatment of the industrial working class. In this work, he uses vitriol and satire to illustrate how this marginalised social stratum was termed "Hands" by the factory owners; that is, not really "people" but rather only appendages of the machines they operated. His writings inspired others, in particular journalists and political figures, to address such problems of class oppression. For example, the prison scenes in *The Pickwick Papers* are claimed to have been influential in having the [Fleet Prison](/wiki/Fleet_Prison "Fleet Prison") shut down. [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") asserted that Dickens "issued to the world more political and social truths than have been uttered by all the professional politicians, publicists and moralists put together".[[232]](#cite_note-KucichSadoff-235) [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw") even remarked that *Great Expectations* was more seditious than Marx's *[Das Kapital](/wiki/Das_Kapital "Das Kapital")*.[[232]](#cite_note-KucichSadoff-235) The exceptional popularity of Dickens's novels, even those with socially oppositional themes (*Bleak House*, 1853; *Little Dorrit*, 1857; *Our Mutual Friend*, 1865), not only underscored his ability to create compelling storylines and unforgettable characters, but also ensured that the Victorian public confronted issues of social justice that had commonly been ignored. *Bleak House*, a satire of protracted legal cases with *[Jarndyce and Jarndyce](/wiki/Jarndyce_and_Jarndyce "Jarndyce and Jarndyce")*—a fictional long-running [Chancery](/wiki/Court_of_Chancery "Court of Chancery") case which has been cited by courts as a symbol of a legal case that interminably drags on—the central plot of the novel, helped support a judicial reform movement that culminated in the enactment of [legal reform](/wiki/Judicature_Acts "Judicature Acts") in England in the 1870s.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

It has been argued that his technique of flooding his narratives with an 'unruly superfluity of material' that, in the gradual dénouement, yields up an unsuspected order, influenced the organisation of [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")'s *[On the Origin of Species](/wiki/On_the_Origin_of_Species "On the Origin of Species")*.[[234]](#cite_note-237)

### Literary techniques

Dickens is often described as using idealised characters and highly sentimental scenes to contrast with his [caricatures](/wiki/Caricature "Caricature") and the ugly social truths he reveals. The story of Nell Trent in *The Old Curiosity Shop* (1841) was received as extremely moving by contemporary readers but viewed as ludicrously sentimental by [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde"). "One must have a heart of stone to read the death of little Nell", he said in a famous remark, "without dissolving into tears ... of laughter."[[235]](#cite_note-238)[[236]](#cite_note-239) [G. K. Chesterton](/wiki/G._K._Chesterton "G. K. Chesterton") stated, "It is not the death of little Nell, but the life of little Nell, that I object to", arguing that the maudlin effect of his description of her life owed much to the gregarious nature of Dickens's grief, his "despotic" use of people's feelings to move them to tears in works like this.[[237]](#cite_note-240)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Tiny-tim-dickens.jpg/250px-Tiny-tim-dickens.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiny-tim-dickens.jpg)

Less fortunate characters, such as Tiny Tim (held aloft by Bob Cratchit), are often used by Dickens in sentimental ways.

The question as to whether Dickens belongs to the tradition of the [sentimental novel](/wiki/Sentimental_novel "Sentimental novel") is debatable. Valerie Purton, in her book *Dickens and the Sentimental Tradition*, sees him continuing aspects of this tradition, and argues that his "sentimental scenes and characters [are] as crucial to the overall power of the novels as his darker or comic figures and scenes", and that "*Dombey and Son* is [ ... ] Dickens's greatest triumph in the sentimentalist tradition".[[238]](#cite_note-241) The *Encyclopædia Britannica* online comments that, despite "patches of emotional excess", such as the reported death of Tiny Tim in *A Christmas Carol* (1843), "Dickens cannot really be termed a sentimental novelist".[[239]](#cite_note-242)

In *Oliver Twist*, Dickens provides readers with an idealised portrait of a boy so inherently and unrealistically good that his values are never subverted by either brutal orphanages or coerced involvement in a gang of young [pickpockets](/wiki/Pickpocketing "Pickpocketing"). While later novels also centre on idealised characters (Esther Summerson in *Bleak House* and Amy Dorrit in *Little Dorrit*), this idealism serves only to highlight Dickens's goal of poignant social commentary. Dickens's fiction, reflecting what he believed to be true of his own life, makes frequent use of coincidence, either for comic effect or to emphasise the idea of providence.[[240]](#cite_note-243) For example, Oliver Twist turns out to be the lost nephew of the upper-class family that rescues him from the dangers of the pickpocket group. Such coincidences are a staple of 18th-century picaresque novels, such as Henry Fielding's *[Tom Jones](/wiki/The_History_of_Tom_Jones,_a_Foundling "The History of Tom Jones, a Foundling")*, which Dickens enjoyed reading as a youth.[[241]](#cite_note-244)

## Reputation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Ottawa_Public_Library.jpg/250px-Ottawa_Public_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_Public_Library.jpg)

Dickens's portrait (top left), in between Shakespeare and [Alfred, Lord Tennyson](/wiki/Alfred,_Lord_Tennyson "Alfred, Lord Tennyson"), on a stained glass window at the [Ottawa Public Library](/wiki/Ottawa_Public_Library "Ottawa Public Library"), Ottawa, Canada

Dickens was the most popular novelist of his time,[[242]](#cite_note-245) and remains one of the best-known and most-read of English authors. His works have never gone [out of print](/wiki/Out-of-print_book "Out-of-print book"),[[243]](#cite_note-246) and have been adapted continually for the screen since the invention of cinema,[[244]](#cite_note-247) with at least 200 motion pictures and television adaptations based on Dickens's works documented.[[245]](#cite_note-248) Many of his works were adapted for the stage during his own lifetime—early productions included *[The Haunted Man](/wiki/The_Haunted_Man_and_the_Ghost%27s_Bargain "The Haunted Man and the Ghost's Bargain")* which was performed in the [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre")'s [Adelphi Theatre](/wiki/Adelphi_Theatre "Adelphi Theatre") in 1848—and, as early as 1901, the British silent film *[Scrooge, or, Marley's Ghost](/wiki/Scrooge,_or,_Marley%27s_Ghost "Scrooge, or, Marley's Ghost")* was made by [Walter R. Booth](/wiki/Walter_R._Booth "Walter R. Booth").[[246]](#cite_note-249) Contemporaries such as the publisher [Edward Lloyd](/wiki/Edward_Lloyd_(publisher) "Edward Lloyd (publisher)") cashed in on Dickens's popularity with cheap imitations of his novels, resulting in his own popular '[penny dreadfuls](/wiki/Penny_dreadful "Penny dreadful")'.[[247]](#cite_note-250)

Dickens created some of the world's best-known fictional characters and is regarded by many as the greatest British novelist of the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era").[[1]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-1) From the beginning of his career in the 1830s, his achievements in English literature were compared to those of Shakespeare.[[193]](#cite_note-Schlicke-195) Dickens's literary reputation, however, began to decline with the publication of *Bleak House* in 1852–53. Philip Collins calls *Bleak House* "a crucial item in the history of Dickens's reputation". Reviewers and literary figures during the 1850s, 1860s and 1870s, saw a "drear decline" in Dickens,[[248]](#cite_note-251) from a writer of "bright sunny comedy ... to dark and serious social commentary".[[249]](#cite_note-252) *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")* called *Bleak House* "a heavy book to read through at once ... dull and wearisome as a serial"; Richard Simpson, in *[The Rambler](/wiki/The_Rambler "The Rambler")*, characterised *Hard Times* as "this dreary framework"; *[Fraser's Magazine](/wiki/Fraser%27s_Magazine "Fraser's Magazine")* thought *Little Dorrit* "decidedly the worst of his novels".[[250]](#cite_note-auto-253) All the same, despite these "increasing reservations amongst reviewers and the chattering classes, 'the public never deserted its favourite'". Dickens's popular reputation remained unchanged, sales continued to rise, and *[Household Words](/wiki/Household_Words "Household Words")* and later *[All the Year Round](/wiki/All_the_Year_Round "All the Year Round")* were highly successful.[[250]](#cite_note-auto-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Charles_Dickens%2C_public_reading%2C_1867.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens%2C_public_reading%2C_1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens,_public_reading,_1867.jpg)

"Charles Dickens as he appears when reading." Wood engraving from *[Harper's Weekly](/wiki/Harper%27s_Weekly "Harper's Weekly")*, 7 December 1867.

As his career progressed, Dickens's fame and the demand for his public readings were unparalleled. In 1868, *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* wrote, "Amid all the variety of 'readings', those of Mr Charles Dickens stand alone."[[10]](#cite_note-Garratt-10) A Dickens biographer, Edgar Johnson, wrote: "It was [always] more than a reading; it was an extraordinary exhibition of acting that seized upon its auditors with a mesmeric possession."[[10]](#cite_note-Garratt-10) The author [David Lodge](/wiki/David_Lodge_(author) "David Lodge (author)") called him the "first writer to be an object of unrelenting public interest and adulation".[[251]](#cite_note-Celebrity-254) Juliet John backed the claim for Dickens "to be called the first self-made global media star of the age of mass culture".[[251]](#cite_note-Celebrity-254) The word "celebrity" first appeared in the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* in 1851, and the BBC states "Charles Dickens was one of the first figures to be called one".[[252]](#cite_note-Dickens_reception-255) Comparing his reception at public readings to those of a contemporary pop star—the BBC compared his reception in the US to [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")—*The Guardian* states, "People sometimes fainted at his shows. His performances even saw the rise of that modern phenomenon, the 'speculator' or [ticket tout](/wiki/Ticket_resale "Ticket resale") (scalpers)—the ones in New York City escaped detection by borrowing respectable-looking hats from the waiters in nearby restaurants."[[252]](#cite_note-Dickens_reception-255)[[253]](#cite_note-256)

> "Dickens's vocal impersonations of his own characters gave this truth a theatrical form: the public reading tour. No other Victorian could match him for celebrity, earnings, and sheer vocal artistry. The Victorians craved the author's multiple voices: between 1853 and his death in 1870, Dickens performed about 470 times."

—Peter Garratt in *The Guardian* on Dickens's fame and the demand for his public readings[[10]](#cite_note-Garratt-10)

Among fellow writers, there was a range of opinions on Dickens. [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth") thought him a "very talkative, vulgar young person", adding he had not read a line of his work, while the novelist [George Meredith](/wiki/George_Meredith "George Meredith") (1828–1909) found Dickens "intellectually lacking".[[254]](#cite_note-257) In 1888 [Leslie Stephen](/wiki/Leslie_Stephen "Leslie Stephen") commented in the *[Dictionary of National Biography](/wiki/Dictionary_of_National_Biography "Dictionary of National Biography")* that "if literary fame could be safely measured by popularity with the half-educated, Dickens must claim the highest position among English novelists".[[255]](#cite_note-258) [Anthony Trollope](/wiki/Anthony_Trollope "Anthony Trollope")'s *Autobiography* famously declared Thackeray, not Dickens, to be the greatest novelist of the age. However, both [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy") and [Fyodor Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoyevsky "Fyodor Dostoyevsky") were admirers. Dostoyevsky commented: "We understand Dickens in Russia, I am convinced, almost as well as the English, perhaps even with all the nuances. It may well be that we love him no less than his compatriots do. And yet how original is Dickens, and how very English!"[[256]](#cite_note-259) Tolstoy referred to *David Copperfield* as his favourite book, and he later adopted the novel as "a model for his own autobiographical reflections".[[257]](#cite_note-Inimitable_Dickens-260) The French writer [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne") called Dickens his favourite writer, writing his novels "stand alone, dwarfing all others by their amazing power and felicity of expression".[[258]](#cite_note-261) Dutch painter [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh") was inspired by Dickens's novels in several of his paintings, such as *Vincent's Chair*, and in an 1889 letter to his sister stated that reading Dickens, especially *A Christmas Carol*, was one of the things that was keeping him from committing suicide.[[259]](#cite_note-262) Oscar Wilde generally disparaged his depiction of character, while admiring his gift for caricature.[[260]](#cite_note-263) Henry James denied him a premier position, calling him "the greatest of superficial novelists": Dickens failed to endow his characters with psychological depth, and the novels, "loose baggy monsters",[[261]](#cite_note-264) betrayed a "cavalier organisation".[[262]](#cite_note-265) [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad") described his own childhood in bleak Dickensian terms, noting he had "an intense and unreasoning affection" for *Bleak House* dating back to his boyhood. The novel influenced his own gloomy portrait of London in *[The Secret Agent](/wiki/The_Secret_Agent "The Secret Agent")* (1907).[[257]](#cite_note-Inimitable_Dickens-260) [Virginia Woolf](/wiki/Virginia_Woolf "Virginia Woolf") had a love-hate relationship with Dickens, finding his novels "mesmerizing" while reproving him for his sentimentalism and a commonplace style.[[263]](#cite_note-266)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/1969_-_Eric_Theater_-_9_Jun_MC_-_Allentown_PA.jpg/250px-1969_-_Eric_Theater_-_9_Jun_MC_-_Allentown_PA.jpg)](/wiki/File:1969_-_Eric_Theater_-_9_Jun_MC_-_Allentown_PA.jpg)

Advert for the Best Picture Oscar winner *[Oliver!](/wiki/Oliver!_(film) "Oliver! (film)")* (1968), an adaptation of *Oliver Twist* and one of over 200 works based on Dickens's novels

Around 1940–41, the attitude of the literary critics began to warm towards Dickens—led by [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell") in *[Inside the Whale and Other Essays](/wiki/Inside_the_Whale_and_Other_Essays "Inside the Whale and Other Essays")* (March 1940), [Edmund Wilson](/wiki/Edmund_Wilson "Edmund Wilson") in *The Wound and the Bow* (1941) and Humphry House in *Dickens and His World*.[[264]](#cite_note-267) However, even in 1948, [F. R. Leavis](/wiki/F._R._Leavis "F. R. Leavis"), in *[The Great Tradition](/wiki/The_Great_Tradition "The Great Tradition")*, asserted that "the adult mind doesn't as a rule find in Dickens a challenge to an unusual and sustained seriousness"; Dickens was indeed a great genius, "but the genius was that of a great entertainer",[[265]](#cite_note-268) though he later changed his opinion with *Dickens the Novelist* (1970, with [Q. D. (Queenie) Leavis](/wiki/Q._D._Leavis "Q. D. Leavis")): "Our purpose", they wrote, "is to enforce as unanswerably as possible the conviction that Dickens was one of the greatest of creative writers".[[266]](#cite_note-269) In 1944 the Soviet film director and film theorist [Sergei Eisenstein](/wiki/Sergei_Eisenstein "Sergei Eisenstein") wrote an essay on Dickens's influence on cinema, such as [cross-cutting](/wiki/Cross-cutting "Cross-cutting")—where two stories run alongside each other, as seen in novels such as *Oliver Twist*.[[267]](#cite_note-270)

In the 1950s, "a substantial reassessment and re-editing of the works began, and critics found his finest artistry and greatest depth to be in the later novels: *Bleak House*, *Little Dorrit* and *Great Expectations*—and (less unanimously) in *Hard Times* and *Our Mutual Friend*".[[268]](#cite_note-271) Dickens was among the favourite authors of [Roald Dahl](/wiki/Roald_Dahl "Roald Dahl"); the best-selling children's author would include three of Dickens's novels among those read by the [title character](/wiki/Matilda_Wormwood "Matilda Wormwood") in his 1988 novel *[Matilda](/wiki/Matilda_(novel) "Matilda (novel)")*.[[269]](#cite_note-272) In 2005, [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), an avid reader of Dickens, named *Nicholas Nickleby* his favourite novel. On Dickens he states, "I like the world that he takes me to. I like his words; I like the language", adding, "A lot of my stuff—it's kind of Dickensian."[[270]](#cite_note-273) The screenwriter [Jonathan Nolan](/wiki/Jonathan_Nolan "Jonathan Nolan")'s screenplay for *[The Dark Knight Rises](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight_Rises "The Dark Knight Rises")* (2012) was inspired by *A Tale of Two Cities*, with Nolan calling the depiction of Paris in the novel "one of the most harrowing portraits of a relatable, recognisable civilisation that completely folded to pieces".[[271]](#cite_note-274) On 7 February 2012, the 200th anniversary of Dickens's birth, [Philip Womack](/wiki/Philip_Womack "Philip Womack") wrote in *The Telegraph*: "Today there is no escaping Charles Dickens. Not that there has ever been much chance of that before. He has a deep, peculiar hold upon us".[[272]](#cite_note-275)

## Legacy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Dickens_and_Nell_Philly.JPG/250px-Dickens_and_Nell_Philly.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dickens_and_Nell_Philly.JPG)

[*Dickens and Little Nell*](/wiki/Dickens_and_Little_Nell_(Elwell) "Dickens and Little Nell (Elwell)") statue in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), Pennsylvania

Museums and festivals celebrating Dickens's life and works exist in many places with which Dickens was associated. These include the [Charles Dickens Museum](/wiki/Charles_Dickens_Museum "Charles Dickens Museum") in London, the historic home where he wrote *Oliver Twist*, *The Pickwick Papers* and *Nicholas Nickleby*; and the [Charles Dickens' Birthplace Museum](/wiki/Charles_Dickens%27_Birthplace_Museum "Charles Dickens' Birthplace Museum") in Portsmouth, the house in which he was born.[[273]](#cite_note-276)[[274]](#cite_note-277) The original manuscripts of many of his novels, as well as printers' proofs, first editions and illustrations from the collection of Dickens's friend John Forster are held at the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum").[[275]](#cite_note-278) Dickens's will stipulated that no memorial be erected in his honour—"I conjure to my friends on no account to make me the subject of any monument, memorial or testimonial whatsoever. I rest my claims to the remembrance of my country upon my published works".[[276]](#cite_note-279) Nonetheless, a life-size bronze statue of Dickens entitled *[Dickens and Little Nell](/wiki/Dickens_and_Little_Nell_(Elwell) "Dickens and Little Nell (Elwell)")*, cast in 1890 by [Francis Edwin Elwell](/wiki/Francis_Edwin_Elwell "Francis Edwin Elwell"), stands in [Clark Park](/wiki/Clark_Park "Clark Park") in the [Spruce Hill](/wiki/Spruce_Hill,_Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania "Spruce Hill, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania") neighbourhood of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), United States. Another life-size statue of Dickens is located at [Centennial Park](/wiki/Centennial_Park,_New_South_Wales "Centennial Park, New South Wales") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia.[[277]](#cite_note-280) In 1960 a [bas-relief](/wiki/Bas-relief "Bas-relief") sculpture of Dickens, notably featuring characters from his books, was commissioned from the sculptor Estcourt J Clack to adorn the office building built on the site of his former home at 1 Devonshire Terrace, London.[[278]](#cite_note-281) In 2014, a life-size statue was unveiled near his birthplace in Portsmouth on the 202nd anniversary of his birth; this was supported by his great-great-grandsons, Ian and [Gerald Dickens](/wiki/Gerald_Charles_Dickens_(actor) "Gerald Charles Dickens (actor)").[[279]](#cite_note-282)[[280]](#cite_note-283)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Christmas_Dinner_Setting.jpg/250px-Christmas_Dinner_Setting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christmas_Dinner_Setting.jpg)

*A Christmas Carol* significantly influenced the modern celebration of Christmas in many countries.

*A Christmas Carol* is most probably his best-known story, with frequent new adaptations. It is also the most-filmed of Dickens's stories, with many versions dating from the early years of cinema.[[281]](#cite_note-Callow2009p39-284) According to the historian [Ronald Hutton](/wiki/Ronald_Hutton "Ronald Hutton") the current state of the observance of Christmas is largely the result of a mid-Victorian revival of the holiday spearheaded by *A Christmas Carol*. Dickens catalysed the emerging Christmas as a family-centred festival of generosity, in contrast to the dwindling community-based and church-centred observations, as new middle-class expectations arose.[[282]](#cite_note-285) Its archetypal figures (Scrooge, Tiny Tim, the Christmas ghosts) entered into Western cultural consciousness. "[Merry Christmas](/wiki/Christmas_and_holiday_season#History_of_the_phrase "Christmas and holiday season")", a prominent phrase from the tale, was popularised following the appearance of the story.[[283]](#cite_note-286) The term Scrooge became a synonym for miser, and his exclamation ["Bah! Humbug!'"](/wiki/Humbug "Humbug"), a dismissal of the festive spirit, likewise gained currency as an idiom.[[284]](#cite_note-287) The Victorian-era novelist [William Makepeace Thackeray](/wiki/William_Makepeace_Thackeray "William Makepeace Thackeray") called the book "a national benefit, and to every man and woman who reads it a personal kindness".[[281]](#cite_note-Callow2009p39-284)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Charles_Dickens_statue%2C_Portsmouth_-_2023-04-21.jpg/250px-Charles_Dickens_statue%2C_Portsmouth_-_2023-04-21.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Dickens_statue,_Portsmouth_-_2023-04-21.jpg)

A statue of Dickens in his birthplace [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), Hampshire

Dickens was commemorated on the [£10 note](/wiki/Banknotes_of_the_pound_sterling "Banknotes of the pound sterling") issued by the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England_note_issues "Bank of England note issues") that circulated between 1992 and 2003. His portrait appeared on the reverse of the note accompanied by a scene from *The Pickwick Papers*.[[285]](#cite_note-288) [The Charles Dickens School](/wiki/The_Charles_Dickens_School "The Charles Dickens School") is a high school in Broadstairs, Kent.[[286]](#cite_note-289) A theme park, [Dickens World](/wiki/Dickens_World "Dickens World"), was open in [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Kent "Chatham, Kent") from 2007 to 2016. To celebrate the 200th anniversary of his birth in 2012, the [Museum of London](/wiki/Museum_of_London "Museum of London") held the UK's first major exhibition on the author in 40 years.[[287]](#cite_note-290) From September 2011 to February 2012, the [Morgan Library & Museum](/wiki/Morgan_Library_%26_Museum "Morgan Library & Museum") in New York celebrated him with its exhibition *Charles Dickens at 200* with manuscripts, photographs, and original illustrations.[[288]](#cite_note-291)

In 2002 Dickens was number 41 in the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s poll of the [100 Greatest Britons](/wiki/100_Greatest_Britons "100 Greatest Britons").[[289]](#cite_note-292) American literary critic [Harold Bloom](/wiki/Harold_Bloom "Harold Bloom") placed Dickens among the [greatest Western writers of all time](/wiki/The_Western_Canon:_The_Books_and_School_of_the_Ages "The Western Canon: The Books and School of the Ages").[[290]](#cite_note-293) In the 2003 UK survey [The Big Read](/wiki/The_Big_Read "The Big Read") carried out by the BBC, five of Dickens's books were named in the [Top 100](/wiki/The_Big_Read#Top_200_novels_in_the_United_Kingdom "The Big Read").[[291]](#cite_note-294)

Actors who have portrayed Dickens on screen include [Anthony Hopkins](/wiki/Anthony_Hopkins "Anthony Hopkins"), [Derek Jacobi](/wiki/Derek_Jacobi "Derek Jacobi"), [Simon Callow](/wiki/Simon_Callow "Simon Callow"), [Dan Stevens](/wiki/Dan_Stevens "Dan Stevens") and [Ralph Fiennes](/wiki/Ralph_Fiennes "Ralph Fiennes"), the latter playing him in *[The Invisible Woman](/wiki/The_Invisible_Woman_(2013_film) "The Invisible Woman (2013 film)")* (2013) which depicts Dickens's alleged secret love affair with Ellen Ternan which lasted for thirteen years until his death in 1870.[[292]](#cite_note-295)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Rus_Stamp_Dickens.jpg/250px-Rus_Stamp_Dickens.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rus_Stamp_Dickens.jpg)

A Soviet postage stamp commemorating Dickens

Dickens and his publications have appeared on a number of postage stamps in countries including: the United Kingdom (1970, 1993, 2011 and 2012 issued by the [Royal Mail](/wiki/Royal_Mail "Royal Mail")—their [2012 collection](/wiki/Great_Britain_commemorative_stamps_2010%E2%80%932019#2012 "Great Britain commemorative stamps 2010–2019") marked the bicentenary of Dickens's birth),[[293]](#cite_note-296) the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (1962), Antigua, Barbuda, Botswana, Cameroon, Dubai, [Fujairah](/wiki/Fujairah "Fujairah"), Saint Lucia and the [Turks and Caicos Islands](/wiki/Turks_and_Caicos_Islands "Turks and Caicos Islands") (1970), [Saint Vincent](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines") (1987), [Nevis](/wiki/Nevis "Nevis") (2007), [Alderney](/wiki/List_of_postage_stamps_of_Alderney "List of postage stamps of Alderney"), Gibraltar, Jersey and [Pitcairn Islands](/wiki/Pitcairn_Islands "Pitcairn Islands") (2012), Austria (2013) and Mozambique (2014).[[294]](#cite_note-297) In 1976 [a crater](/wiki/Dickens_(crater) "Dickens (crater)") on the planet [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)") was named in his honour.[[295]](#cite_note-298)

In November 2018 it was reported that a previously lost portrait of a 31-year-old Dickens, by [Margaret Gillies](/wiki/Margaret_Gillies "Margaret Gillies"), had been found in [Pietermaritzburg](/wiki/Pietermaritzburg "Pietermaritzburg"), South Africa. Gillies was an early supporter of [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") and had painted the portrait in late 1843 when Dickens, aged 31, wrote *A Christmas Carol*. It was exhibited, to acclaim, at the [Royal Academy of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts") in 1844.[[88]](#cite_note-Brown-89) The [Charles Dickens Museum](/wiki/Charles_Dickens_Museum "Charles Dickens Museum") is reported to have paid £180,000 for the portrait.[[296]](#cite_note-299)

## Works

Main article: [Charles Dickens bibliography](/wiki/Charles_Dickens_bibliography "Charles Dickens bibliography")

Dickens published 15 major novels, several novellas, a large number of short stories (including a number of Christmas-themed stories), a handful of plays and several non-fiction books.

### Novels and novellas

Most of Dickens's work was initially published in serial form, either weekly in magazines or monthly in free-standing instalments, then reprinted in standard book formats.

* *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")* (*The Posthumous Papers of the Pickwick Club*; monthly serial, April 1836 to November 1837).[[297]](#cite_note-300) Novel.
* *[Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist "Oliver Twist")* (*The Adventures of Oliver Twist*; monthly serial in *[Bentley's Miscellany](/wiki/Bentley%27s_Miscellany "Bentley's Miscellany")*, February 1837 to April 1839). Novel.
* *[Nicholas Nickleby](/wiki/Nicholas_Nickleby "Nicholas Nickleby")* (*The Life and Adventures of Nicholas Nickleby*; monthly serial, April 1838 to October 1839). Novel.
* *[The Old Curiosity Shop](/wiki/The_Old_Curiosity_Shop "The Old Curiosity Shop")* (weekly serial in *[Master Humphrey's Clock](/wiki/Master_Humphrey%27s_Clock "Master Humphrey's Clock")*, April 1840 to November 1841). Novel.
* *[Barnaby Rudge](/wiki/Barnaby_Rudge "Barnaby Rudge")* (*Barnaby Rudge: A Tale of the Riots of Eighty*; weekly serial in *[Master Humphrey's Clock](/wiki/Master_Humphrey%27s_Clock "Master Humphrey's Clock")*, February to November 1841). Novel.
* *[Martin Chuzzlewit](/wiki/Martin_Chuzzlewit "Martin Chuzzlewit")* (*The Life and Adventures of Martin Chuzzlewit*; monthly serial, January 1843 to July 1844). Novel.
* *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol "A Christmas Carol")* (*A Christmas Carol in Prose: Being a Ghost-story of Christmas*; 1843). Novella.
* *[The Chimes](/wiki/The_Chimes "The Chimes")* (*The Chimes: A Goblin Story of Some Bells That Rang an Old Year Out and a New Year In*; 1844). Novella.
* *[The Cricket on the Hearth](/wiki/The_Cricket_on_the_Hearth "The Cricket on the Hearth")* (*The Cricket on the Hearth: A Fairy Tale of Home*; 1845). Novella.
* *[Dombey and Son](/wiki/Dombey_and_Son "Dombey and Son")* (*Dealings with the Firm of Dombey and Son: Wholesale, Retail and for Exportation*; monthly serial, October 1846 to April 1848). Novel.
* *[The Battle of Life](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Life "The Battle of Life")* (*The Battle of Life: A Love Story*; 1846). Novella.
* *[The Haunted Man](/wiki/The_Haunted_Man_and_the_Ghost%27s_Bargain "The Haunted Man and the Ghost's Bargain")* (*The Haunted Man and the Ghost's Bargain: A Fancy for Christmas-time*; 1848). Novella.
* *[David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield "David Copperfield")* (*The Personal History, Adventures, Experience and Observation of David Copperfield the Younger of Blunderstone Rookery [Which He Never Meant to Publish on Any Account]*; monthly serial, May 1849 to November 1850). Novel.
* *[Bleak House](/wiki/Bleak_House "Bleak House")* (monthly serial, March 1852 to September 1853). Novel.
* *[Hard Times](/wiki/Hard_Times_(novel) "Hard Times (novel)")* (*Hard Times: For These Times*; weekly serial in *[Household Words](/wiki/Household_Words "Household Words")*, 1 April 1854, to 12 August 1854). Novel.
* *[Little Dorrit](/wiki/Little_Dorrit "Little Dorrit")* (monthly serial, December 1855 to June 1857). Novel.
* *[A Tale of Two Cities](/wiki/A_Tale_of_Two_Cities "A Tale of Two Cities")* (weekly serial in *[All the Year Round](/wiki/All_the_Year_Round "All the Year Round")*, 30 April 1859, to 26 November 1859). Novel.
* *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations "Great Expectations")* (weekly serial in *[All the Year Round](/wiki/All_the_Year_Round "All the Year Round")*, 1 December 1860 to 3 August 1861). Novel.
* *[Our Mutual Friend](/wiki/Our_Mutual_Friend "Our Mutual Friend")* (monthly serial, May 1864 to November 1865). Novel.
* *[The Mystery of Edwin Drood](/wiki/The_Mystery_of_Edwin_Drood "The Mystery of Edwin Drood")* (monthly serial, April 1870 to September 1870). Novel. Left unfinished due to Dickens's death.

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** [John Forster](/wiki/John_Forster_(biographer) "John Forster (biographer)") quotes an unpublished letter in which Dickens responds to the accusation that he must not have seen Grimaldi in person: "Now, Sir, although I was brought up from remote country parts in the dark ages of 1819 and 1820 to behold the splendour of Christmas pantomimes and the humour of Joe, in whose honour I am informed I clapped my hands with great precocity, and although I even saw him act in the remote times of 1823 ... I am willing ... to concede that I had not arrived at man's estate when Grimaldi left the stage".[[19]](#cite_note-Forster65-19) When Dickens arrived in America for the first time in 1842, he stayed at the [Tremont House](/wiki/Tremont_House_(Boston) "Tremont House (Boston)"), America's "pioneer first-class hotel". Dickens "bounded into the Tremont's foyer shouting out 'Here we are!', Grimaldi's famous catch-phrase and as such entirely appropriate for a great and cherished entertainer making his entrance upon a new stage."[[20]](#cite_note-Slater178-20) Later, Dickens was known to imitate Grimaldi's clowning on several occasions.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-181)** A contemporary obituary in *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, alleged that Dickens's last words were: "Be natural my children. For the writer that is natural has fulfilled all the rules of Art." Reprinted from *The Times*, London, August 1870 in [Bidwell 1870](#CITEREFBidwell1870), p. 223.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-219)** Slater also detects Ellen Ternan in the portrayal of Lucie Manette.
